courses.title: Called to Completion: Leading with Integrity, Faith & Excellence in Esther Funds Foundation
courses.description: Called to Completion is the official Esther Funds Foundation leadership training and certification course for executive board members, ambassadors, and chapter founders. Rooted in the Book of Nehemiah, this course equips students to lead with spiritual integrity, organizational excellence, and faith-based purpose. You'll learn chapter operations, financial management, event planning, ministry integration, and more. Each module includes lessons, quizzes, and practical tools to guide your leadership journey. Certification is awarded after completing the course and final exam. All leaders must complete this course to serve.

modules.title: Module 2 – The Heart of Our Mission
Module 4 – Code of Conduct & Spiritual Accountability
MODULE 5: Mastering Branding, Marketing & Public Representation
Hear From Tamara – President, EFF UNCC
Module 8 – Faith in Leadership & Ministry Integration
Module 9 – Membership, Recruitment & The Esther’s Experience
Module 11: Maximizing Chapter Resources & Tools for Impact

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Welcome to the Leadership Course
lessons.markdown:
# 01-lesson-61433404

Welcome to the Esther Funds Foundation Leadership Training Course!

You made it. You’ve officially stepped into national leadership—and that means more than just a title. You’re part of a faith-driven movement to keep students in school and walking in purpose.

Whether you're a President, Fundraising Chair, or Social Media Lead—this course will equip you with the tools to lead with excellence, integrity, and fire.

And don't worry... we’re not here to waste your time. This course is short, interactive, and packed with what you actually need to know.

You’ll learn:


What your role really means
How to handle finances and fundraising the right way
How to plan powerful events and stay compliant
How to lead spiritually, not just organizationally
You're not just running a chapter. You’re helping rebuild the future for students who almost gave up.

Let’s build something holy, bold, and lasting.

lessons.title: How This Course Works + Accommodations
lessons.markdown:
# 02-lesson-61433633

Let’s keep it real: nobody wants to sit through a boring training. This course was created so you can actually learn what matters—without feeling like you're back in class.

Here’s what to expect:


You’ll walk through 13 short modules (they don’t drag—we promise).
At the end of each module, there’s a quick quiz to help you retain what you just learned.
There’s a final exam at the end to receive your certification.
Once you pass, you’ll be added to our National Leader Registry and officially recognized as an EFF-certified leader.
You’ll have 14 days to complete the course from the day you had your leadership session with the National team. That gives you plenty of time to pace yourself—about 2 modules per day. Training must be completed by June 17, 2025.

This course includes reading, reflection, and decision-making. If you need more time because of a disability or learning difference, don’t hesitate to reach out. We're here to support you.

Email: info@estherfundsfoundation.org Subject Line: Leadership Course 

Here’s the bottom line: This isn’t school. This isn’t busywork. This is about your calling, and the real-life impact you’re about to make on your campus and in the lives of students who need what you carry

# 04-lesson-61483034

Reminder: This is a certification course. That means you’re not just here to scroll, click, and disappear.

Once you complete this full course (modules + final exam): ✅ You’ll be officially certified as an Esther Funds Foundation leader ✅ You’ll receive a digital certificate ✅ Your certification will be valid for 1 year from your completion date ✅ You’ll earn the right to say, “I’m trained, trusted, and tapped in.”

lessons.title: The Burden to Build
lessons.markdown:
# 05-lesson-61433728

Nehemiah wasn’t a pastor.

He wasn’t a prophet.He was just someone who cared enough to act.

He saw something broken—and said, “I’ll build.”

That’s what made him a leader. Not a title. Not a platform. A burden.

And that’s what makes you a leader in Esther Funds Foundation. You saw something on your campus, in your life, or in this world—and felt the pull to help rebuild futures.

📖 Bible Verse to Reflect On: “Then I said to them, ‘You see the trouble we are in... Come, let us rebuild the wall of Jerusalem, and we will no longer be in disgrace.’” – Nehemiah 2:17 (NIV)
If you’d like to learn more about Nehemiah’s story, take 3 minutes to read Nehemiah Chapter 1 when you get a chance.

lessons.title: Leadership Starts with Prayer, Not Performance
lessons.markdown:
# 06-lesson-61433744

Nehemiah didn’t lead from ego. He didn’t build to be seen.

He prayed. He fasted. He planned. And he moved when God said move.

EFF leaders are called to lead with that same posture.

We’re not building chapters just to host events. We’re building so that students can finish what they started—spiritually, emotionally, and academically.

Let’s keep it simple: Pray first. Plan well. Persevere always.

Before You Go

Let this be your foundation as a leader:

You don’t need to be perfect. You just need to be willing. And if God called you to it, He’ll help you build it.

lessons.title: What You Just Stepped Into
lessons.markdown:
# 08-lesson-61435590

You didn’t just accept a position. You accepted a mission.

Esther Funds Foundation (EFF) isn’t just a student organization. It’s a faith-driven national nonprofit on a Kingdom assignment: to help students stay in school and finish well—spiritually, academically, emotionally, and financially.

We are the collegiate version of the National Dropout Prevention Center—but we lead with prayer, purpose, and people.

Here’s what makes us different:


We are Christ-centered

We are student-led

We are impact-driven

We are rooted in the story of Esther — rising up “for such a time as this.”
💭 You were chosen to lead, not to perform—but to protect purpose on your campus.

lessons.title: What Makes EFF Different
lessons.markdown:
# 09-lesson-61463442

Here’s what sets us apart:


We are Christ-centered: Faith isn’t separate from what we do—it’s the foundation.
We are student-led: Chapters are run by students for students.
We are impact-driven: Every event, meeting, and moment is focused on helping people finish.
We are a nonprofit, meaning we operate for purpose, not profit. And we are a ministry, meaning we serve students not just academically, but spiritually.

Every Bible study, service project, and workshop is part of something holy.

Our Slogan – "Every Future Fulfilled"

Our slogan, "Every Future Fulfilled," encapsulates our commitment to ensuring that every student, regardless of their background, has the opportunity and support to complete their education and achieve their dreams. This guiding principle drives all our initiatives and programs.

questions.question: What is the official slogan of Esther Funds Foundation?
answers: “No Student Left Behind”
Graduation Over Everything”
“Every Future Fulfilled”
“Finish the Race, Keep the Faith”
correct_answers: “Every Future Fulfilled”

lessons.title: Leadership Is Stewardship
lessons.markdown:
# 10-lesson-61463454

As a leader within EFF, your role is one of stewardship, not status. This means:



Serving with humility: Recognizing that leadership is about serving others and meeting their needs.

Leading with integrity: Upholding ethical standards and being a role model for others.

Empowering others: Encouraging and supporting team members to reach their full potential.

Being accountable: Taking responsibility for your actions and decisions.
Your leadership is a trust placed in you to guide and support students on their educational journey.

EFF operates as a Christ-centered nonprofit ministry dedicated to preventing college dropouts by providing emergency support, educational resources, and faith-based encouragement. Our approach combines practical assistance with spiritual guidance, addressing both the tangible and intangible challenges students face.

We believe that higher education should not be a privilege but an accessible reality for all students, especially those facing financial hardship and a lack of support. Through our programs, we aim to eliminate financial barriers and provide the necessary support, knowledge, and faith to help students finish what they started.

questions.question: As a leader in Esther Funds Foundation, what does your role represent?
answers: a) Popularity and visibility on campus
b) A temporary role with no long-term responsibility
c) Stewardship — serving with humility, integrity, and accountability
d) Control over chapter decisions and creative freedom
correct_answers: c) Stewardship — serving with humility, integrity, and accountability

lessons.title: The Origin Story
lessons.markdown:
# 11-lesson-61435598

Esther Funds Foundation (EFF) was founded on January 23, 2024, by Shayna Vincent, a college student from Tampa, Florida, majoring in Elementary Education at Florida A&M University. Shayna faced significant challenges during her academic journey, including ADHD and behavioral issues, which led to multiple instances of dropping out. These personal experiences ignited a passion in her to support other students facing similar obstacles.

Recognizing the systemic barriers that underrepresented, first-generation, and low-income college students encounter, Shayna established EFF to provide emergency support, educational resources, and faith-based encouragement. The foundation began as a vision in 2023 to break financial and systemic barriers that hinder students from completing their education.

What started at Florida A&M University became a movement:


Over a dozen chapters
Scholarship funding for students in crisis
Emergency support, Bible studies, advocacy, and leadership
And now—you.

If you're reading this, you are part of the next chapter of this story.

📖 Esther 4:14 —And who knows but that you have come to your position for such a time as this?”

Mission: We work to prevent college dropouts in the United States by providing emergency support to minority, first-generation, and low-income college students—especially in the South—who face financial and systemic barriers to completing their education. Through advocacy, mentoring, service, and donations, we increase the graduation rate and equip underrepresented college students with the immediate resources, long-term knowledge, and faith-based support they need to persist and graduate.

Vision: We envision a world where underrepresented, first-generation, and low-income students graduate at higher rates, achieve financial stability, and secure equitable representation in every field of the workforce. Our goal is to help 7 million underrepresented college students graduate—gaining the economic and educational knowledge needed to break generational cycles of poverty, empowering them to build a future of success for themselves and those who come after them.

Shayna Vincent

Founder & Executive Director

EFF Colors & National Mascot

Part of representing EFF well means understanding the meaning behind the brand—why we use certain colors, what the dove represents, and how it connects to our mission and message.

EFF Official Colors

💜 Royal Purple


Represents royalty, purpose, and identity

Inspired by Queen Esther’s calling in the Bible
Reminds every student that no matter their past, they are chosen and worthy

⚪ White


Symbolizes purity, healing, and new beginnings

Reflects God’s grace and the restoration that happens when students are given the support they need to stay in school and walk in purpose
🕊️ EFF National Mascot: The Dove


EFF’s official symbol is the dove, representing:


Peace in the middle of pressure
Purity and protection

The Holy Spirit, which guides everything we do as a Christ-centered ministry

Gentle strength — we’re not loud to be effective; we’re grounded to be impactful
The dove is a reminder that even in the chaos of college, God’s presence, peace, and provision are near. Every flyer, wristband, and shirt with the dove is a symbol of hope, safety, and calling.

lessons.title: Module 1 Activity

# 12-lesson-61477183

Module 1 Activity: Mission & Vision Reflection

📍 As a leader, it’s important to know the mission and vision of EFF. It is required to read both aloud at the start of every General Body Meeting and every Executive Board Meeting.

📜 Mission of Esther Funds Foundation


We work to prevent college dropouts in the United States by providing emergency support to minority, first-generation, and low-income college students—especially in the South—who face financial and systemic barriers to completing their education. Through advocacy, mentoring, service, and donations, we increase the graduation rate and equip underrepresented college students with the immediate resources, long-term knowledge, and faith-based support they need to persist and graduate. 

🌍 Vision of Esther Funds Foundation


We envision a world where minority, first-generation, and low-income students graduate at higher rates, achieve financial stability, and secure equitable representation in every field of the workforce. Our goal is to help 7 million underrepresented college students graduate—gaining the economic and educational knowledge needed to break generational cycles of poverty, empowering them to build a future of success for themselves and those who come after them.

lessons.title: This Course Will Equip You
lessons.markdown:
# 14-lesson-61463460

Navigating This Course

Congratulations! You just completed your first quiz! This leadership training course is designed to equip you with the knowledge and skills necessary to effectively lead within EFF. Here's how to navigate it:



Modules: The course consists of 13 short modules, each focusing on different aspects of leadership and EFF operations.

Pacing: Aim to complete 2 modules per day, finishing the course within 14 days of your onboarding session.

Quizzes: At the end of each module, there will be a short quiz to reinforce your learning.

Final Exam: Upon completing all modules, you'll take a final exam to earn your certification.

Support: If you have a learning disability or learning difference, please email us at info@estherfundsfoundation.org with the subject line: "Leadership Course Request."
Remember, this course is not just about acquiring knowledge—it's about preparing to make a meaningful impact in the lives of students on your campus!

# 15-lesson-61479950

EFF has an official hand sign that represents both the heart and the dove? 🕊️💜

The EFF hand sign is made by:


Creating a heart shape with your hands 💜
Crossing your fingers gently within it to symbolize the dove’s wings

It’s more than just a cute photo pose.

The EFF hand sign represents:


Love + peace
Identity + covering

The heart of Esther Funds Foundation: gentle strength, unity, and purpose
💡 Every time you throw it up in a picture or at an event, remember what (and who) you represent.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Why College Dropout Prevention Matters
lessons.markdown:
# 16-lesson-61463583

Why College Dropout Prevention Matters

Too many students don’t make it to graduation—not because they’re not smart, but because life hits hard.

Students are dropping out every year because:


They can’t afford tuition, food, or housing
They’re overwhelmed by mental health struggles
They’re the first in their family to go to college and don’t know where to turn
They’re carrying adult-sized responsibilities with little support
EFF exists to say: you don’t have to do this alone.

We're here to intervene early, offer support, speak life, and walk with students so they don’t just survive college—they finish it.

This is why what you’re building matters. It's not just about membership. It’s about miracles in motion.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Who We Serve
lessons.markdown:
# 17-lesson-61463589

Esther Funds Foundation is for the students who feel like giving up. Specifically:


First-generation college students
Low-income students
Black and Brown students facing systemic barriers
Working students trying to juggle it all
Students raising children, dealing with trauma, or navigating chronic stress
Students who want more than academic help—they want hope

We’re here to stand in the gap—practically and spiritually.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Faith + Equity + Retention
lessons.markdown:
# 18-lesson-61463595

EFF blends faith, equity, and retention into everything we do. That means:


We pray and we provide
We support the whole student—mind, body, and spirit
We advocate for students who are overlooked or forgotten
We believe your GPA matters—but so does your well-being and your walk with God
This isn’t about putting religion on people—it’s about serving with love and boldness, the way Jesus did.

We believe college graduation can be a Kingdom victory.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Our Four National Initiatives
lessons.markdown:
# 19-lesson-61463698

Everything EFF does flows through four national initiatives—each one designed to meet a specific need in the student journey.

1. The Campus Ambassador Program

This is our outreach and student voice initiative. Campus Ambassadors are the hands and feet of EFF on their campus.

This program includes:



Ambassadors, who spread the mission, host drives, and lead initiatives

Royal Courts, a creative extension of the Ambassador Program that blends student leadership, advocacy, and campus representation
If your chapter plans to have a Royal Court, you’ll want to refer to the Esther Funds Foundation National Royal Court Policy and Guidelines, located in the Chapter Resources section. (We’ll go deeper into this later in the course.)

2. The James Pillar Project

This is where every collegiate chapter operates.

Named after the Book of James (wisdom + faith in action), this initiative focuses on:


Academic and professional development
Mentorship
Student success workshops
Soft skill training and career prep
If you're leading a chapter on campus, this is your zone. Everything from your events to your impact reports falls under this project.

3. The EFF Lift Fund

This is our emergency support vision. Our long-term goal is to officially launch the EFF Lift Fund, a national emergency fund to provide:


Micro-grants for groceries, gas, or textbooks
Transportation and housing aid
Critical retention resources
While some chapters may already give in crisis situations, our national Lift Fund is still being developed. You’re helping lay the foundation.

4. Esther’s Light Ministry

This is the spiritual heart of EFF. Esther’s Light is our national ministry initiative focused on discipleship, Scripture, and healing.

Each chapter’s Director of Collegiate Ministry runs this initiative locally, through:


Bible studies
Prayer nights
Devotionals and spiritual check-ins
We’ll go deeper into what this looks like later in the course, but just know: you are leading a real ministry—not just an organization.

These four initiatives keep us focused, mission-aligned, and organized as we grow.

Together, they reflect the pillars of EFF: Presence. Preparation. Provision. Prayer.

lessons.title: Module 2 Activity: The Heart of Our Mission
lessons.markdown:
# 20-lesson-61477403

Module 2 Activity: Scenario-Based Mission Application

📍This activity will help you apply what you learned about EFF’s mission and the students we serve. Read each scenario and respond with how your chapter should respond — based on the heart of our mission.

Scenario 1: A Student on the Verge of Dropping Out

You meet a first-generation student on campus who shares that they’re considering dropping out due to financial stress, lack of support, and academic burnout. They’ve stopped attending class and don’t know who to talk to.

Scenario 2: Planning an Event That Honors the Mission

Your chapter is planning an event and someone suggests a casual hangout night with no clear purpose—just food and music. It’s easy, fun, and low effort.

Scenario 3: Campus Doesn’t Understand What EFF Does

You’re tabling at your school’s student org fair and someone asks, “So is EFF just like a Bible study club?”

lessons.title: Did you know? #1
lessons.markdown:
# 22-lesson-61478317

Did You Know? EFF Was Featured on the News!

🎥 Did you know Esther Funds Foundation was featured on WCTV? That’s right — our movement to prevent college dropouts made the news!

EFF was recognized for its innovative approach to supporting students through emergency resources, mentorship, faith, and leadership. What started at one campus is now a national nonprofit with growing chapters across the country.

💬 This isn’t just a campus org. This is a national ministry—and the world is watching.
You are now a part of something that’s bigger than you—and making real headlines. Lead accordingly.

lessons.title: MODULE 3 – Executive Board Roles & Chapter Structure
lessons.markdown:
# 23-lesson-61463705

This module walks through the leadership positions that make up an EFF chapter, how the board should function, and how to protect order, clarity, and alignment across campuses.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Makes a Strong Chapter?
lessons.markdown:
# 24-lesson-61463707

What Makes a Strong Chapter?

A strong EFF chapter isn’t about popularity—it’s about purpose and order.

Every EFF chapter must:


Have a full executive board in place
Assign clear roles and responsibilities
Operate in alignment with national policies and structure
Submit updates and communicate with HQ regularly
We’re not running casual clubs. We are leading ministry-based nonprofits on campus. That means structure protects the mission.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Executive Board Positions & Responsibilities
lessons.markdown:
# 25-lesson-61463710

Full Executive Board & Leadership Structure

Every chapter of Esther Funds Foundation must be built on spiritual alignment, professional order, and shared responsibility. Below is a breakdown of all required, recommended, and extended leadership roles, including their expectations.

President


Leads the chapter with spiritual grounding, order, and vision
Oversees all chapter operations, events, and board communication
Hosts executive board and general body meetings
Serves as the main liaison to National Headquarters
Upholds national policy, mission, and branding standards
Makes final decisions with maturity and prayer
Vice President(s) – VP 1 and VP 2


Supports the President and steps in when needed
Oversees e-board communication and semester planning
Helps track goals, accountability, and campus impact
Plays a major role in executing workshops and The Esther’s Experience
Coordinates internal structure (VP 1) and programming (VP 2) as needed
 Secretary



Takes minutes during all board and general meetings
Organizes all chapter documents, contact lists, and meeting records
Maintains folders (Google Drive, Canva, reports, etc.)
Assists with distributing reminders, recaps, and compliance documents
 Treasurer / Financial Chair



Manages all chapter funds, fundraising records, and spending
Logs all financial activity and submits reports each semester
Works with HQ and university to remain compliant
Collaborates with Fundraising Chair on every campaign
 Director of Collegiate Ministry



Leads the chapter spiritually through prayer, Bible study, and devotionals
Oversees the Esther’s Light Ministry initiative locally
Checks in spiritually with members and board
Coordinates prayer events, faith reflections, and Scripture posts
Membership Chair


Leads all recruitment efforts and onboarding of new members
Plans and hosts interest meetings and orientations
Oversees The Esther’s Experience
Manages attendance, member lists, and retention
Fundraising Chair


Plans, executes, and tracks fundraising events
Partners with Treasurer for proper financial reporting
Leads creativity in campus-based and online fundraisers
Helps chapters reach scholarship, event, and service goals
 Community Service Chair



Coordinates all volunteer efforts and donation drives
Partners with organizations for service opportunities
Logs all volunteer hours and submits documentation
Aligns service with EFF’s dropout prevention mission
 Public Relations / Social Media Chair



Manages the chapter’s social media and digital presence
Creates flyers, announcements, and post captions using approved branding
Promotes events, recruitment, and national campaigns
Responds to DM inquiries and oversees professional public image
 Parliamentarian



Maintains order and structure during all meetings
Assists President in enforcing Robert’s Rules of Order
Supports voting, agenda control, and respectful board conduct
Historian


Documents chapter activities through photos, highlights, and archives
Assists PR Chair with recap posts and visual storytelling
Submits key milestones and media for national newsletter or website features
Volunteer Manager


Tracks and calculates all volunteer hours for each event
Submits a full Volunteer Report to HQ each semester
Works with Community Service Chair and Secretary to log hours accurately
Ensures individual and chapter-wide totals are submitted by deadline

🟦 Extended Leadership Support Roles

These roles may not be on the executive board but are still nationally recognized as part of your leadership team.

🟪 Advisor / Co-Advisor (Faculty or Staff)



Ensures chapter is in good standing with the university
Supports the executive board with oversight and guidance
Attends major events and meetings (as able)
Reviews the semester compliance packet before submission
May be asked to verify fundraising, conflict resolution, or discipline
🟪 National Chapter Liaison (Assigned by HQ)



Serves as your direct support from National HQ
Helps track chapter progress, answer questions, and offer accountability
Checks in monthly or as needed
Supports conflict resolution, compliance, and leadership transitions
Keeps communication flowing between HQ and the chapter
🟪 Campus Ambassador



Represents EFF through social media, outreach, or fundraising
Participates in national campaigns and donor engagement
May or may not be part of the chapter’s executive board
Required to complete Ambassador training and participate in 1–2 initiatives per semester
🟪 Royal Court Member / Royal Court Ambassador



Participates in campus representation and chapter visibility
Often serves during interest meetings, social events, and scholarship campaigns
May hold special duties during induction, fundraisers, and collabs
Expected to embody EFF values and attend leadership-based events
💡 Note: Every leader is part of a national movement. Titles reflect responsibility, but your character reflects the mission.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Chapter Structure & Chain of Command
lessons.markdown:
# 26-lesson-61463740

Chapter Structure & Chain of Command

Your EFF chapter is part of a nationally structured nonprofit—not an independent student club. That means you operate under clear accountability and order.

Here’s how the chain of command works:

National Headquarters (HQ)

Oversees all chapters, initiatives, policies, and structureChapter’s National Liaison (if assigned)


Serves as the direct point of contact between your chapter and National HQ
Helps monitor progress, offer support, and communicate updates
Chapter Executive Board


President, Vice Presidents, and other board roles carry out all day-to-day operations on campus
Responsible for following national policies and submitting required reports
General Body Members

Attend events, engage in service, participate in programming, and contribute to chapter cultureVolunteers and Participants

May attend one-time events or help on a limited basis but are not official members unless inducted

🚨 What to Do If There’s an Issue

If a conflict arises or something goes wrong in your chapter:



Try to resolve it within the board. Private conversation > group chat drama.
If needed, bring in your chapter advisor.

Then contact your National Liaison if one is assigned.
If it’s still unresolved or serious (ex: legal, ethical, or spiritual violations), reach out to National HQ directly.

No gossip. No group chat explosions. No rebellion. Follow the order—and let God honor your maturity and submission to structure.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Leadership ≠ Friendship
lessons.markdown:
# 27-lesson-61463810

This part matters.

Leading EFF doesn’t mean picking your best friends or holding on to positions because of loyalty. That’s how confusion and conflict happen.

Every person on your board must:


Be active (not just on the roster)
Be committed to the mission
Be teachable and willing to grow
Respect the spiritual and professional tone of EFF
If someone isn't contributing, you have the authority—and the responsibility—to make changes. We’ll go into this more in the Code of Conduct module.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Preparing for Transitions
lessons.markdown:
# 28-lesson-61463815

Preparing for Transitions

Strong chapters don’t just run—they transition well.

Every executive board role in Esther Funds Foundation is a position of stewardship, and it must be treated with integrity from start to finish.

Leadership Terms & Expectations


All executive board members are considered active for up to one academic year after training and certification.
New leaders must complete national leadership training and pass the certification exam before officially serving.
Chapters are required to have all core roles filled (President, VP, Treasurer, etc.) to remain in good standing.
If your chapter fails to maintain an executive board:


You may be placed on CND (Cease & Desist) status

You may lose access to events, recruitment, or funding
National HQ may request a formal meeting or audit
🧭 How to Bring on New E-Board Members Mid-Year


If a position becomes vacant or your chapter is expanding leadership, there are 3 approved ways to bring on new board members:



Interviews: The chapter hosts interviews and selects the best-fit candidate with board approval.

Majority Vote: The chapter takes a formal vote during an e-board or GBM meeting.

Elections: Candidates campaign and speak; voting is opened to members.
💡 All leadership selections must be clearly documented—names, roles, dates, and method of selection must be saved for compliance.

📝 National Auditing


At any time, National HQ has the right to audit your executive board, especially if:


Concerns arise
Multiple reports are missing
Chapter growth is stalled
Uncertified leaders are serving in official roles
Make sure your board is always in alignment, trained, and active.

📥 What to Include in a Transition Plan


Before the semester ends or a role is handed off:


Host a transition meeting
Provide passwords, templates, and chapter materials
Share role expectations and documentation practices
Submit a leadership roster update to HQ if changes occur mid-year
Support incoming leaders spiritually, practically, and professionally

lessons.title: Module 3 Activity: Know Your Role – Leading With Clarity
lessons.markdown:
# 29-lesson-61477891

Every successful EFF chapter runs on clear structure, communication, and shared leadership. Use this activity to reflect on your role and understand how to work with others on your board.

“The Late Flyer Situation”

Your chapter is hosting a Bible Study on Thursday night. It’s already Tuesday evening and the flyer still hasn’t been posted. The President is frustrated, the PR Chair says they never received the event details, and the Secretary isn’t sure what’s been confirmed. Tensions are high, and now the whole board is scrambling.

b) Parliamentarian
c) Community Service Chair and or Volunteer Manager

answers: a) Membership Chair

b) Handles flyer design for your chapter

d) Leads your chapter’s GBMs

lessons.title: MODULE 4 – Code of Conduct & Spiritual Accountability
lessons.markdown:
# 31-lesson-61463925

This module breaks down how you are expected to lead, what behaviors are unacceptable, and how accountability is handled inside Esther Funds Foundation.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What We Expect from You as a Leader
lessons.markdown:
# 32-lesson-61463928

What We Expect from You as a Leader

As an EFF leader, you are a representative of:


Christ
Our national mission
Your chapter’s culture
That means:


Leading with humility, not ego
Practicing professionalism at all times
Serving others, not using your title as a pass
Showing up to meetings, events, and initiatives prepared and on time
Being spiritually grounded and emotionally mature

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Boundaries in Leadership
lessons.markdown:
# 33-lesson-61463929

EFF leaders are called to protect the emotional, spiritual, and physical safety of others. This means respecting:


Personal space and boundaries
Confidentiality in sensitive conversations
No favoritism or cliques
No romantic abuse of power
No inappropriate jokes, posts, or DMs
Boundaries also include knowing when to step back, take a break, or ask for help. Burnt-out, bitter leadership is not holy leadership.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Our Code of Conduct – What’s Not Allowed
lessons.markdown:
# 34-lesson-61463930

The Esther Funds Foundation Code of Conduct outlines the national expectations for all members, leaders, and affiliates. It is a binding policy that must be reviewed and agreed to by every executive board member and inducted member.

📍 Where to Find It:


You can view the full Code of Conduct at any time by visiting: ➡️ www.estherfundsfoundation.org Then click on “Chapter Resources” and locate the file titled: 🔒 Esther Funds Foundation National Code of Conduct

🚨 Every member must:


Adhere to the document in full
Agree to it when applying or being inducted
Uphold it in person and online
This is not optional. Violations of the Code may result in warnings, probation, or immediate removal.

These actions can result in immediate removal and potential reporting:

🚫 Hazing 🚫 Rebellion or mutiny against national policies 🚫 Fund misuse or financial dishonesty 🚫 Disrespecting national liaisons, advisors, or executive team 🚫 Cyberbullying, public defamation, or toxic leadership 🚫 Inappropriate DMs, sexual misconduct, or spiritual manipulation

EFF is a safe space. If your actions make others feel unsafe, unsupported, or uncomfortable—there will be consequences.

assets.name: Esther Funds Foundation National Code of Condu (1).pdf

lessons.title: Lesson 4: What Happens if the Code is Violated
lessons.markdown:
# 35-lesson-61463932

What Happens if the Code is Violated

Discipline within EFF is not about shame—it’s about protecting the mission.

Steps of the process:



Initial documentation (verbal or written complaint submitted)
Review by the chapter advisor and/or liaison
Official warning or probation (if needed)
Removal from leadership or membership (if serious or unresolved)
Each case is handled with confidentiality, professionalism, and prayer.

You don’t need to be perfect—but you must be accountable.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Grace and Growth Still Matter
lessons.markdown:
# 36-lesson-61463936

Grace and Growth Still Matter

EFF believes in restoration. If you make a mistake, don’t hide it—grow through it.


Apologize when needed
Accept correction with humility
Take spiritual inventory of your heart
Commit to learning and improving
We believe in second chances—but we don’t ignore serious harm.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Character Over Clout
lessons.markdown:
# 37-lesson-61463985

Your position doesn’t make you powerful—your posture does.

As a leader:


Be above reproach
Stay teachable
Keep your character clean even when no one’s watching
Ask for accountability and spiritual covering
Remember: you’re not just building a chapter—you’re building credibility, legacy, and trust.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: How to Report a Code of Conduct Violation
lessons.markdown:
# 38-lesson-61464010

How to Report a Code of Conduct Violation

If someone violates the code of conduct:


Don’t handle it on your own. There is a process in place to protect everyone involved.Here’s how to report:


Go to the Esther Funds Foundation website

Navigate to the “Chapter Resources” section

Click the “Code of Conduct Violation Form”

Fill out all required fields with detailed, honest information
You can report:


Misconduct by an EFF leader or member
Situations of harm, abuse, or boundary crossing
Financial or ethical concerns
Repeated disrespect or rebellion
Reports go directly to National HQ and are reviewed with full discretion. Retaliation against someone who reports is strictly prohibited and will result in removal.

If you are unsure whether something counts as a violation—report it anyway. It’s better to ask than ignore it.

lessons.title: Quiz
lessons.markdown:
# 39-lesson-61464014

lessons.title: MODULE 5 – Branding, Marketing & Public Representation
lessons.markdown:
# 40-lesson-61464058

Branding, Marketing & Public Representation

Core Goal: Equip every chapter leader to become a professional, Spirit-led brand ambassador for EFF — on social media, on campus, and in real life. Branding is our first impression, and as a ministry, it must reflect purpose, excellence, and integrity.

This is one of the most important modules in this entire training.

Why? Because you are the first impression of EFF—online, on campus, and in your community. You are the post. You are the caption. You are the invite. And you’re also the follow-up.

How you present EFF matters. People will decide whether to trust the mission based on how you post, speak, design, and carry yourself. This module will guide you through official branding, social media expectations, flyer design rules, and how to represent the organization well in every space.

📢 Representation Matters. You’re not just representing an org—you’re representing a movement, a ministry, and a mission.

Every post, caption, outfit, and conversation reflects Esther Funds Foundation. Branding = stewardship.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Representing the Brand with Excellence
lessons.markdown:
# 41-lesson-61464080

Representing the Brand with Excellence

You are not just representing a campus org—you’re representing a national nonprofit and ministry.

Everything you post, say, or create in relation to EFF must reflect:


Professionalism
Spiritual integrity
Clear, mission-driven messaging
This includes:


Flyers
Social media posts
Emails
Collaborations
Announcements
Your personal behavior while wearing merch or using the name

Why Representation Matters

Whether you post on your story, wear your EFF shirt, or speak at a campus event—you are representing a national nonprofit and a faith-based ministry.

This means:


What you post matters.
How you carry yourself matters.
The language you use matters.
And the way you show up reflects not only EFF, but the God we serve.

💬 “Let your light shine before others, so they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven.” – Matthew 5:16

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Official EFF Branding Rules (This is Non-Negotiable)
lessons.markdown:
# 42-lesson-61464086

Lesson 2: Official Branding Rules

Every chapter must follow Esther Funds Foundation’s National Brand Guidelines.

📍 You can find the full brand guide under the Chapter Resources section on our website.

Here are the basics:

✅ USE:


Your official chapter logo provided by HQ
The Royal Purple and White color palette
League Spartan and Libre Baskerville fonts
Transparent logo PNGs sent to your email or available on request
National taglines: “Every Future Fulfilled”

🚫 DO NOT:


Recolor or redesign the logo
Use national logos for chapter-only events
Create knockoff flyers with non-approved fonts, random colors, or distorted logos
Add mascots or club branding to EFF materials
Post graphics using outdated or off-brand templates
If you're ever unsure, email us at info@estherfundsfoundation.org for approval.

EFF has a clear, powerful, and consistent visual identity—and every chapter is expected to follow it. This protects the brand, builds trust, and sets you apart as a national movement—not just another club.

✅ Branding Rules (You Must Follow):


Only use official EFF logos, fonts, and colors

Never stretch, recolor, or redesign EFF logos
All flyers must include the purple dove and follow the official palette
Use templates from the Chapter Resource Hub
Avoid personal branding that conflicts with or overpowers EFF visuals

Download & Review These Branding Resources:

assets.name: Esther Funds Foundation Official Brand Guideli.pdf
Esther Funds Foundation Branding, Social Media.pdf

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Flyer Design & Posting Expectations
lessons.markdown:
# 43-lesson-61464094

Flyer Design & Posting Expectations

All flyers should:


Be clean, clear, and professional
Include the chapter-specific logo, not the national one
Be spell-checked
Include correct times, dates, locations, and contact info
Reflect a tone of encouragement, purpose, and honor—not chaos or sloppiness
Flyers should never include:


Curse words, inappropriate jokes, or trendy content that goes against EFF values
Last-minute low-quality graphics
Confusing or misleading language
Want help? Use Canva templates we’ve created or request support from the national PR team.

Use the Social Media Kits – We Made This Easy For You

You do NOT need to reinvent the wheel. You have full access to:


Pre-made flyer templates
Canva story templates
Captions you can adjust for your chapter
Branded graphics for events, fundraisers, Bible studies, and more
Where to Find Them:

Visit the Chapter Resource Hub and download the full EFF Social Media Kit + Flyer Templates Folder

Your chapter should not be posting off-brand flyers, low-res images, or random colors. You’re running a national ministry—so it needs to look like it.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Social Media Expectations
lessons.markdown:
# 44-lesson-61464095

Social Media Expectations

Your chapter’s social media page is public-facing. It reflects our values.

That means:


You must post at least 2x a month

You must avoid posting EFF events on your personal page only

You must manage DMs in a timely and respectful way
All major flyers should be approved by your President and posted professionally
Avoid oversharing memes, TikToks, or trending sounds that don’t reflect the mission
If someone comments negatively—don’t argue online. Bring it to your President or national liaison.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Collaborations and Co-Hosting
lessons.markdown:
# 45-lesson-61464100

Collaborations and Co-Hosting

Your chapter can collaborate with other orgs on or off campus—but you must follow these rules:

✅ DO:


Use your chapter logo
Maintain EFF’s values in all event language and design
Get approval from your chapter President before agreeing
Ensure you still represent our mission (not just hype)
🚫 DON’T:


Co-host with businesses that conflict with EFF values (ex: alcohol-focused bars, clubs, or inappropriate brands)
Let another org take over the flyer
Use collabs as a way to skip over chapter responsibilities
All collabs must align with EFF’s mission: dropout prevention, student success, and Christ-centered support

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Crisis Communication
lessons.markdown:
# 46-lesson-61464104

Crisis Communication

If there’s ever an issue with:


A social media post that goes viral
A complaint or crisis reported to EFF
A student or leader saying something inappropriate publicly
Here’s what to do:


Pause. Don’t react emotionally.
Screenshot or save evidence.
Report the situation to your chapter President and advisor.
Contact your national liaison or HQ if it’s serious.
Do NOT post apologies, stories, or responses without national guidance. We are a legally trademarked organization—every response must be professional and aligned.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Social Media & PR Roles – Lead the Brand
lessons.markdown:
# 47-lesson-61491128

🧑🏽‍💻 PR Chair Responsibilities:


Design branded flyers using official colors, logos, and templates
Manage the chapter’s Instagram and digital presence
Write captions that reflect the mission
Post consistently with quality and clarity
Coordinate with other board members to promote events
📸 Social Media Chair or Committee:


Schedule weekly content
Use kits and templates from HQ
Create reels, stories, and engagement-based posts
Report insights and engagement during board meetings

lessons.title: Lesson 8: Merchandise & Brand Integrity
lessons.markdown:
# 48-lesson-61491130

Merchandise & Brand Integrity

EFF merch is part of the movement—but it must be ordered, posted, and worn correctly.

✅ All merch must:


Be approved and/or purchased through the national merch store or bulk order form

Use official colors and logos only
Never be modified or reproduced independently
➡️ You cannot make your own merch without national approval.

All approved merch can be found on store at estherfundsfoundation.store

questions.question: Where can approved EFF merchandise be found?
answers: estherfundsfoundation.store
estherfundsfoundation.org
estherfundsfoundation.com
estherfundsfoundation.co
correct_answers: estherfundsfoundation.store

lessons.title: Lesson 9: Social Media Do's and Don'ts
lessons.markdown:
# 49-lesson-61491169

Social Media Do’s & Don’ts

Because how you post matters.

When someone sees your flyer or story post, they’re not just seeing a design—they’re seeing our mission, ministry, and movement. One off-brand flyer can hurt your credibility more than you realize.

This lesson will help you stay spirit-led AND brand-aligned in every post.

✅ DO’s – Every Post Should Reflect These Standards:




Double-check spelling — No typos, no lazy grammar. Proofread before you post.

Use official EFF colors only — Royal purple, white, black, soft lavender (ONLY when approved).


Use white text on dark purple backgrounds — It’s readable, clean, and professional.

Include your chapter name on every flyer — Not just “Esther organization.” Be specific: Esther Funds Foundation at [Your University]


Use the purple dove logo — Use it in its official form. Never recolor, stretch, or crop it.

Choose clean music — No profanity, no alcohol references, no sexually suggestive content in reels or stories. You’re representing a ministry.

Check your design contrast — White on dark purple, black on white. No pink text. No unreadable neon on neon.

Save shield logo for formal or major chapter documents (e.g., induction, press, or partnership decks) — not casual flyers.
❌ DON’Ts – Even One of These Can Undermine Your Message:



Don’t use random shades of purple (no light purples, lavenders, or pastels unless part of an official template).
Don’t post flyers without your chapter name AND the correct logo.
Don’t call EFF “Esther Club” or “Esther organization.” Our full name is Esther Funds Foundation.
Don’t repost viral content that conflicts with our values—even on your personal page if you’re in leadership.
Don’t blast graphics that haven’t been reviewed by your PR Chair or President.
Don’t pick music with curse words, sexual content, or party/drug themes for posts.
Don’t use your personal branding to override our national branding. This is about mission, not ego.

questions.question: What must every chapter include on all flyers and chapter related shirts?
answers: a) Just the dove logo
b) The phrase “Esther Club”
c) Their full chapter name and official EFF branding
d) Their favorite Bible verse
correct_answers: c) Their full chapter name and official EFF branding

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT allowed in EFF merchandise design?
answers: a) Custom chapter shirt using approved brand palette
b) Recolored shield logo in pastel pink
c) Using the official purple dove
d) Ordering through the national bulk merch form
correct_answers: b) Recolored shield logo in pastel pink

questions.question: When is it appropriate to use the EFF shield logo?
answers: a) On every casual post and IG story
b) Only on formal documents
c) On Canva mockups before approval
d) For group chat memes
correct_answers: b) Only on formal documents

questions.question: Why is it important to order merch through the national store or approved process?
answers: a) So it looks better in pictures
b) To maintain brand consistency, avoid legal violations, and honor EFF’s visual integrity
c) To compete with other orgs
d) Because HQ said so
correct_answers: b) To maintain brand consistency, avoid legal violations, and honor EFF’s visual integrity

lessons.title: Lesson 9 Activity: Merch Request Walkthrough
lessons.markdown:
# 50-lesson-61491208

Lesson 9 Activity: Merch Request Walkthrough

Your chapter wants to create custom t-shirts for an upcoming event. You want them to look great, match your campus vibe, and still represent EFF the right way.

Before you jump into Canva or place an order, let’s walk through what needs to happen first.

lessons.title: Hear From Tamara – President, EFF UNCC
lessons.markdown:
# 51-lesson-61478900

“The biggest thing for me was connection.”“I think the number one thing for me was making a connection with people who were interested in joining. I already knew a lot of people because of my other leadership roles, but I made sure not to limit myself to people I was already connected with.I reached out to people I didn’t know at all—and I let them know this isn’t just about joining a group. It’s about being seen, heard, and supported.One thing I’ve learned is that members grow when they feel like the President actually knows them—not just the e-board. I’ve heard so many people say they’ve been in orgs where the President never connected with them... and it made them feel invisible. That won’t happen here.”“EFF is really ‘come as you are.’”“I always remind people—this isn’t a perfect Christian club. You don’t need to have your spiritual life together to show up.Whether you’re figuring out your relationship with God, or you’re literally on the verge of dropping out, you’re welcome here.Our job is to support your growth—not to judge your starting point.”

lessons.title: MODULE 6 – Financial Stewardship & Fundraising Ethics
lessons.markdown:
# 52-lesson-61464285

Financial Stewardship & Fundraising Ethics

Handling money in EFF is not just a job—it’s a trust. When a student donates, when a business gives, or when someone supports your fundraiser, they’re trusting you to be transparent, responsible, and honest.

This module will teach you the basics of chapter finance, fundraising do’s and don’ts, and how to keep everything clean and reportable.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Financial Roles in the Chapter
lessons.markdown:
# 53-lesson-61464292

Financial Roles in the Chapter

There are three key financial leaders in your chapter:

🟪 Treasurer



Tracks all money in and out
Logs every transaction with receipts
Submits semesterly financial reports
Works closely with the President and advisor
Uses only approved platforms and methods
🟪 Fundraising Chair



Plans and executes fundraisers
Promotes giving campaigns and coordinates with the PR team
Helps track participation and results
Collaborates with the Treasurer to ensure funds are documented properly
🟪 President



Oversees and approves all spending
Ensures fundraising is compliant with national rules
Submits updates to HQ if needed
📌 Note: No one should be fundraising without your chapter’s knowledge or record keeping. “Silent” or secret fundraising is prohibited.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Where Chapter Money Can Go
lessons.markdown:
# 54-lesson-61464294

Where Chapter Money Can Go

Here’s what EFF funds can be used for:


Community service supplies (kits, hygiene products, etc.)
Chapter events (food, decorations, printing)
Member materials (Bibles, folders, pens, wristbands)
Marketing tools (flyers, ads, Canva Pro)
Funds should NOT be used for: 🚫 Personal expenses 🚫 Giving money directly to individuals without documentation 🚫 Travel unrelated to EFF 🚫 Unapproved merchandise or giveaways 🚫 Anything outside the mission

All expenses must be trackable and reportable.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Fundraiser Planning & Rules
lessons.markdown:
# 55-lesson-61464345

Fundraiser Planning & Rules

Fundraisers should always be: ✅ Mission-aligned ✅ Approved by your advisor and executive board ✅ Tracked (how much raised, how, and where it’s going) ✅ Transparent to donors and students

Common fundraisers:


Donation drives (clothes, hygiene kits, school supplies)
Online giving campaigns (Cash App, PayPal)
Raffles, merch sales, or challenge-based campaigns (like Double Good Popcorn or Drip Don’t Drop)
🧾 Be sure to keep screenshots of Cash App receipts, PayPal reports, or Google Sheets logs! These will be required for your compliance packet.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Approved Giving Platforms
lessons.markdown:
# 56-lesson-61464371

Approved Giving Platforms

Chapters may use:


Cash App
PayPal
Venmo (business)

Square or GiveButter if approved by your university
All fundraisers must be connected to one official EFF chapter account, not personal student accounts.

Chapter Cash Apps should be labeled clearly, like:


$EFFGrambling or $EstherFundsTXSO
Do NOT use personal Cash Apps for official fundraising.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Financial Red Flags
lessons.markdown:
# 57-lesson-61464374

Financial Red Flags

These are signs something is going wrong:


Fundraisers being run without receipts or tracking
A leader is collecting cash or digital funds and not reporting it
Confusion about where money went
Students questioning where funds are
No one submitting a financial report at semester’s end
If these things are happening: PAUSE EVERYTHING and report to your chapter advisor and HQ. It’s better to get clarity than risk trust.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Semester Financial Reports
lessons.markdown:
# 58-lesson-61464376

Semester Financial Reports

Every chapter must submit a financial stewardship report in the compliance packet. It should include:


List of all fundraisers
How much was raised
What the funds were used for
Screenshots or receipts for proof
Advisor or Treasurer signature
This keeps your chapter in good standing, shows donors you’re trustworthy, and protects your chapter from confusion or future issues.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Fundraising for Chapter-Based Scholarships
lessons.markdown:
# 59-lesson-61480025

Fundraising for Chapter-Based Scholarships

One of the most powerful things your chapter can do is create your own local scholarship to bless a student on your campus. But with great impact comes great responsibility—especially when it involves money.

🎓 Why Offer a Chapter Scholarship?


Creating a scholarship allows your chapter to:


Directly support a student who’s at risk of dropping out
Show donors where their money is going
Represent EFF’s mission in action

Build visibility and trust on campus
Empower your members to give back
💸 Fundraising Dos & Don’ts for Chapter Scholarships


✅ DO:



Plan a dedicated fundraiser with clear scholarship branding
Open a Google Form or application that includes eligibility, GPA, short responses, and deadlines
Collect funds through an official chapter account only (Cash App, PayPal, etc.)
Keep detailed records of how much was raised and how it will be disbursed

Promote the winner with professionalism and prayer

🚫 DON’T:



Choose winners based on popularity or bias
Collect money through a personal account
Announce the scholarship without a plan or process
Use leftover funds for unrelated events
Forget to document and submit the results in your compliance report
📁 How to Stay in Compliance:



Include your scholarship flyer, promo post, winner announcement, and payout receipts in your semester compliance packet
Include the scholarship summary in your fundraising report

Ask your advisor to verify the final selection (optional but recommended)

lessons.title: Module 6 Activity: Stewarding Every Dollar
lessons.markdown:
# 60-lesson-61479967

Module 6 Activity: “Fix That Fundraiser!” 

Your job is to step into these 3 quick mini-scenarios as the Financial Chair or President and decide what went wrong — and how to fix it.

🧩 Scenario 1: The Cash App Confusion


Your chapter raises $300 through a fundraiser, but it was collected through someone’s personal Cash App. Now there’s no clear log of who donated or how it’ll be spent.

Flyer & Funds

You hosted a bake sale but forgot to promote it properly, and no one tracked who paid what. You made money—but can’t prove anything.

questions.question: What should have been done differently?
answers: A) Collect through a chapter-labeled account with screenshots and a tracker
B) Let everyone use their own Cash App to split responsibility
C) Just cash out and Venmo it to the Treasurer later
correct_answers: A) Collect through a chapter-labeled account with screenshots and a tracker

lessons.title: Module 6 Quiz – Financial Stewardship & Fundraising Ethics
lessons.markdown:
# 61-lesson-61464650

Module 6 Quiz – Financial Stewardship & Fundraising Ethics

Passing Score: 80% Instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

questions.question: Which of the following is an approved use of chapter funds?
answers: a) Uber rides for e-board members
b) Gift cards for personal use
c) Purchasing supplies for a hygiene kit drive
d) Birthday lunch for a member
correct_answers: c) Purchasing supplies for a hygiene kit drive

questions.question: What is one platform your chapter can use for fundraising (if clearly labeled and documented)?
answers: a) Personal Cash App
b) Chapter-designated Cash App or PayPal
c) Someone’s parent’s Zelle
d) Personal Venmo
correct_answers: b) Chapter-designated Cash App or PayPal

questions.question: Which of the following would be considered financial misuse?
answers: a) Submitting receipts to your advisor
b) Using chapter funds for groceries for your own home
c) Posting your chapter Cash App publicly for a fundraiser
d) Partnering with your Treasurer on a budget
correct_answers: b) Using chapter funds for groceries for your own home

questions.question: Before you begin a fundraiser, what should always be in place?
answers: a) Snacks and decorations
b) A board vote only
c) A clear plan, chapter approval, and a way to track funds
d) A group chat flyer
correct_answers: c) A clear plan, chapter approval, and a way to track funds

questions.question: What is one of the Treasurer’s responsibilities?
answers: a) Host prayer nights
b) Document all fundraiser income and submit a financial report
c) Recruit members
d) Order merch
correct_answers: b) Document all fundraiser income and submit a financial report

questions.question: Why should your chapter NEVER use a personal account to collect donations?
answers: a) It’s inconvenient
b) It’s a national branding violation
c) It creates confusion, no accountability, and potential for misuse
correct_answers: c) It creates confusion, no accountability, and potential for misuse

questions.question: Which item should NOT be funded with EFF chapter money?
answers: a) Bible Study snacks
b) Canva Pro subscription for flyers
c) Gas money for someone’s trip to Target
d) Hygiene drive supplies
correct_answers: c) Gas money for someone’s trip to Target

questions.question: What should be done at the end of every fundraiser?
answers: a) Celebrate and move on
b) Delete any financial records
c) Submit a flyer of the event
d) Track and log what was raised and where it went
correct_answers: d) Track and log what was raised and where it went

questions.question: What’s a major red flag in chapter fundraising?
answers: a) A late flyer
b) A Treasurer who has no access to the account
c) Using a branded flyer
d) Posting about the fundraiser in GroupMe
correct_answers: b) A Treasurer who has no access to the account

questions.question: What’s an important thing to consider when fundraising for a chapter-based scholarship?
answers: a) Whether you like the applicant
b) Who raised the most money
c) How the scholarship will be tracked, disbursed, and reported
d) What color the flyer is
correct_answers: c) How the scholarship will be tracked, disbursed, and reported

lessons.title: MODULE 7 – Chapter Compliance & Semester Responsibilities
lessons.markdown:
# 62-lesson-61464390

Chapter Compliance & Semester Responsibilities

Being a leader means doing more than showing up. It means making sure your chapter is active, accountable, and in alignment with EFF’s mission. This module walks through what’s required each semester and how to stay in good standing with National Headquarters.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What is Compliance?
lessons.markdown:
# 63-lesson-61464564

Lesson 1: What is Compliance?

Compliance is how we make sure:


Your chapter is doing the work
National can track real impact
You’re eligible for grants, partnerships, awards, and recognition
Every chapter must submit a Compliance Packet at the end of each semester. Think of it like your chapter’s final exam.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Required Semester Activities
lessons.markdown:
# 64-lesson-61464573

Required Semester Activities

To remain in good standing, your chapter must complete the following every semester:

✅ 2 Bible Studies or Faith-Based Discussions ✅ 2 Workshops or Educational Events ✅ 1 Donation Drive ✅ 2 General Body Meetings (GBMs) ✅ Membership Recruitment (if applicable) ✅ 2–3 Collaborations with other organizations ✅ Participation in a National EFF Initiative ✅ 1 Scholarship or Resource-Based Event ✅ Monthly Executive Board Meetings ✅ Active Campus Engagement ✅ Active Social Media Presence (minimum 2 posts/month) ✅ Leadership Training Attendance ✅ Financial Stewardship Report ✅ Advisor Signature Verification

📌 All of this is tracked and submitted through your Compliance Packet.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: How to Document Activities
lessons.markdown:
# 65-lesson-61464591

How to Document Activities

Do not wait until the last week of the semester. Start tracking from day one.

Here’s what to save:


📸 Photos from each event
📄 Flyers or promotional graphics
✅ Sign-in sheets or QR code check-ins
💬 Recaps or reflection notes
📱 Screenshots of social media posts
🧾 Fundraising receipts and Cash App screenshots
🖊 Advisor-signed forms or verification emails
📁 Create a folder named “EFF Compliance – [Your Chapter Name]” and drop all of this in as the semester goes.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: How to Submit the Packet
lessons.markdown:
# 66-lesson-61464596

How to Submit the Packet

Packets are due:


📅 December 15 (Fall Semester)
📅 May 8 (Spring Semester)
Upload it to the official form link (sent via email or group chat), or email to info@estherfundsfoundation.org if instructed otherwise.

Make sure your advisor signs off before submitting.

Late or incomplete packets may result in:


Probation
Ineligibility for fundraising or scholarship support
Temporary pause on recruitment or royal court activity
Deactivation or C&D letter

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Pro Tips for Staying Organized
lessons.markdown:
# 67-lesson-61464613

Pro Tips for Staying Organized

✅ Hold a “compliance meeting” near the end of each semester ✅ Assign each e-board member a section of the form ✅ Create shared folders (Google Drive, OneDrive) for storing event proof ✅ Label your photos and receipts clearly ✅ Ask questions early—not the night before

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Mission-Focused Events (Quick Ideas)
lessons.markdown:
# 68-lesson-61464645

Mission-Focused Events (Quick Ideas)

If you’re stuck on what events to host, here are a few mission-aligned ideas:

Bible Study Topics:


Trusting God in College
Identity in Christ
Mental Health & Scripture
Workshop Ideas:


Finding Scholarships
Budgeting in College
Career Planning for First-Gen Students
Donation Drive Ideas:


Finals Survival Kits
Hygiene Products
Grocery Gift Card Fundraiser
Collab Ideas:


Campus Ministries
Career Centers
Student Government
National Initiative Ideas:


Drip Don’t Drop Challenge
Prayer & Purpose Campaign
First-Gen Celebration Week

lessons.title: Module 7 Activity: Compliance Walkthrough – Protecting Your Chapter
lessons.markdown:
# 69-lesson-61482313

Compliance Walkthrough – Protecting Your Chapter

📍Every active chapter is required to submit a semester compliance packet. This activity will guide you through what’s expected and why it matters—using a visual tool already created just for you.

🔗 Step 1: Open the Compliance Guide Presentation


The full guide is uploaded under Chapter Resources on the Esther Funds Foundation website. You can also click here: https://estherfundsfoundation.org/eff-chapter-resources

👀 Step 2: Browse Through These Key Sections:



Why Compliance Matters
Required Activities Checklist
How to Document Throughout the Semester
Pro Tips & Common Mistakes
Mission-Focused Event Ideas
Final Reminders

lessons.markdown:
# 70-lesson-61464649

Instructions: Choose the best answer. Passing score: 80%.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the semester compliance packet?
answers: a) To apply for graduation
b) To track how many followers your chapter gained
c) To show National HQ that your chapter is active, aligned, and completing required activities
d) To submit designs for national merch
correct_answers: c) To show National HQ that your chapter is active, aligned, and completing required activities

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a required semester activity?
answers: a) A donation drive
b) 2 General Body Meetings
c) 1 Fashion Show
d) Participation in a national initiative
correct_answers: c) 1 Fashion Show

questions.question: When is the semester compliance packet due?
answers: a) December 15 (Fall) and May 8 (Spring)
b) First day of the next semester
c) Whenever your advisor says
d) January 1 and June 1
correct_answers: a) December 15 (Fall) and May 8 (Spring)

questions.question: What should be included in your compliance folder?
answers: a) Personal notes and devotions
b) Budget projections for the next year only
c) Flyers, attendance, recap photos, financial report, and volunteer hour log
d) Only your Instagram highlights
correct_answers: c) Flyers, attendance, recap photos, financial report, and volunteer hour log

questions.question: What happens if your chapter fails to submit a compliance packet?
answers: a) Nothing—it’s optional
b) You may be placed on probation or CND status
c) Your advisor gets promoted

lessons.title: MODULE 8 – Faith in Leadership: Ministry Integration
lessons.markdown:
# 71-lesson-61464671

Faith in Leadership: Ministry Integration

EFF is not just a nonprofit. It’s a ministry. Every leader must understand how to lead spiritually and create an environment where God is welcome, not an afterthought.

This module equips you with tools to make faith part of your leadership, not just something you mention once a semester.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Why Faith Is the Foundation
lessons.markdown:
# 72-lesson-61464676

Why Faith Is the Foundation

Esther Funds Foundation is a Christ-centered organization.

That means:


We pray together
We lead Bible studies
We reflect God’s character in how we serve
We don’t separate spiritual growth from academic success
Your role as a leader includes protecting the spiritual tone of your chapter.

This doesn’t mean forcing religion—it means building a culture that reflects the love, power, and peace of God.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: The Role of the Director of Collegiate Ministry
lessons.markdown:
# 73-lesson-61464680

The Role of the Director of Collegiate Ministry

This position is one of the most important on the board.

The Director of Collegiate Ministry is responsible for:


Hosting Bible studies or prayer nights
Offering spiritual check-ins to the board or members
Running the Esther’s Light Ministry Initiative at the chapter level
Planning faith-based content and supporting events with scripture
Partnering with the President to open/close meetings in prayer
This person must be spiritually mature, prayerful, and grounded in their walk with Christ.

If your chapter doesn’t have this position filled, pray about who’s ready for it—it’s vital to your growth and protection.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: What is Esther’s Light?
lessons.markdown:
# 74-lesson-61464685

What is Esther’s Light?

Esther’s Light is the official national ministry initiative of EFF.

Each chapter runs Esther’s Light locally through:


Bible study series
Scripture devotionals
Instagram faith content
Prayer & Purpose Campaigns
Monthly reflection moments
This initiative ensures that ministry doesn’t get lost in the middle of all the programming.

You’ll hear more about this in later modules, but know now: your chapter should always have a spiritual rhythm.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Required Spiritual Practices for Chapters
lessons.markdown:
# 75-lesson-61464687

Required Spiritual Practices for Chapters

To stay aligned with the mission, each chapter is required to: ✅ Host at least 2 Bible studies or spiritual events per semester ✅ Open executive board meetings with prayer or scripture ✅ Reflect Christ’s love in leadership conversations and decisions ✅ Ensure social media posts reflect spiritual maturity and respect ✅ Create space for students to grow in faith, not just show up for events

This can look different chapter to chapter—but the presence of God must be visible.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Making Room for God in Everything
lessons.markdown:
# 76-lesson-61464697

Making Room for God in Everything

You don’t need to be a pastor or perfect. But as a leader, you must be:


Prayerful
Humble
Open to God’s voice
Bold about your faith
Willing to let God interrupt your plans when needed
Make room for the Holy Spirit in:


Planning meetings
Member gatherings
Unexpected moments on campus
Conversations with discouraged students
The way you respond to conflict
This is Kingdom work. And Kingdom leaders stay close to the King.

lessons.title: Module 8 Activity: Leading with Faith on Your Campus
lessons.markdown:
# 77-lesson-61482411

Leading with Faith on Your Campus

📍In EFF, leadership is ministry, not performance. This activity will help you think about what it means to spiritually lead—not just host events.

✨ Instructions: Choose One Prompt to Complete


Pick one of the following prompts and respond with honesty and heart. No fluff, no perfection required.

Option 1: Think about the students on your campus who feel spiritually disconnected or discouraged. How can your chapter’s Bible studies, devotionals, or presence help them feel seen and loved by God? (Write 3–4 sentences.)

Option 2: Imagine someone joins your chapter who’s unsure about their relationship with God. How would you explain what it means to be part of a Christ-centered nonprofit that still welcomes people where they are? (Write 3–4 sentences.)
