# 80-lesson-61492346

FYI: As we get closer to recruitment season, National Headquarters will host a dedicated meeting to dive deeper into the full recruitment process. While this course provides a strong foundation, we understand everything may not be covered in full detail here — so we’ll walk through things together when the time comes!

lessons.title: MODULE 9 – Membership, Recruitment & The Esther’s Experience
lessons.markdown:
# 81-lesson-61464727

Membership, Recruitment & The Esther’s Experience

Recruitment is not just about growing numbers. In EFF, recruitment is ministry. Every new member is a student whose life you’re about to impact—so it must be done with purpose, planning, and prayer.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Overview of Recruitment
lessons.markdown:
# 82-lesson-61464728

Overview of Recruitment

No chapter can begin recruitment until they’ve:


Received national approval via the Membership Recruitment Planning Form
Have all required executive board roles filled

Gained advisor and university event approval

Completed this leadership training course

Chapters that recruit without approval will be placed on probation.

📍 All recruitment must follow the 2025 National Membership Recruitment Manual found under Chapter Resources.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Required Recruitment Roles
lessons.markdown:
# 83-lesson-61464732

Required Recruitment Roles

Before recruitment begins, these chapter roles must be filled:


President
Vice President(s)
Membership Chair
Director of Collegiate Ministry
Community Service Chair
Fundraising Chair
Treasurer
These roles are essential for supporting every phase of the process—from planning to prayer to service and induction.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Recruitment Process Step-by-Step
lessons.markdown:
# 84-lesson-61464739

Recruitment Process Step-by-Step

Here’s the approved national flow for recruitment:



Submit Planning Form to HQ for approval
(Optional) Host an Informational Session

Host a Mandatory Interest Meeting

Open the Membership Application

Collect and submit all applicant data to HQ
Wait for national approval before sending out Acceptance Letters
Accepted students begin The Esther’s Experience

Hold a formal Induction Ceremony

Members complete national enrollment via Join It

No steps should be skipped. This is a national standard process.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: The Esther’s Experience
lessons.markdown:
# 85-lesson-61464744

The Esther’s Experience

This is the pre-induction process every accepted student must complete before being inducted. It is non-negotiable.

Required Components: ✅ Orientation or Membership Mixer ✅ Bible Study or Prayer Event ✅ Community Service Event ✅ Fundraising Participation ✅ Bonding or Sisterhood Event

This process is not about hazing. It’s about preparing students spiritually, emotionally, and socially for what it means to be part of Esther Funds Foundation.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Hosting a Powerful Interest Meeting
lessons.markdown:
# 86-lesson-61464769

Hosting a Powerful Interest Meeting

The Interest Meeting is the official start of recruitment.

You must:


Clearly explain the mission and expectations of EFF
Review The Esther’s Experience in full
Present the Code of Conduct
Let students know the cost of membership ($30 national fee via GoDaddy)
Provide next steps for applying
It should be professional, organized, and led by the executive board—not just one person talking for an hour.

assets.name: ESTHER FUNDS CHAPTER NAME INTEREST MEETING EXA.pdf

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Application Process
lessons.markdown:
# 87-lesson-61464771

Application Process

All membership applications must:


Be digital (Google Forms preferred)
Collect full name, GPA (min 2.0), and proof of payment
Include Code of Conduct acknowledgment
Stay open no longer than 2 weeks
Once the application closes:


Submit a complete spreadsheet to National HQ
Wait for official approval before sending acceptance letters
Only the President or Vice President may send acceptance letters using the national template.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Induction Ceremony Requirements
lessons.markdown:
# 88-lesson-61464772

Induction Ceremony Requirements

Once students complete The Esther’s Experience, they must be formally inducted.

Induction must include:


Opening and closing prayers
Scripture reading
Charge to new members
National Membership Pledge
Recognition of board and inductees
A professional, faith-filled atmosphere
You can add guest speakers, music, or personal touches—but it must reflect excellence and reverence.

lessons.title: Lesson 8: Final Enrollment
lessons.markdown:
# 89-lesson-61464778

Final Enrollment

After induction, all new members must:


Register through the official national platform, Join It

Complete their digital profile
Receive their digital membership card
This gives them access to national opportunities, scholarships, and recognition.

lessons.title: Lesson 9: Collecting National Membership Fees
lessons.markdown:
# 90-lesson-61482738

National One-Time Induction Fee & Join It Membership

When a student is accepted into Esther Funds Foundation, they are required to pay a one-time national induction fee. This is not optional—it is the only way they become officially recognized as a member of the organization.

💳 How to Pay the National Fee


All national membership dues must be paid through the official EFF website: 👉 www.estherfundsfoundation.org

🚫 Do NOT collect national dues through your chapter Cash App, Venmo, or in person. This is a national policy. Collecting dues this way is a compliance violation and may result in chapter probation.

🟣 Why It Matters: Join It = National Proof of Membership


Once a student pays their national fee through the website, they will be automatically enrolled in EFF’s national membership platform: Join It.

This is how National HQ:


Confirms who is officially inducted
Sends certifications and communications
Tracks national impact
Protects the integrity of our growing member base
📌 If a student is not in Join It, they are not recognized as a member—no matter what events they attend.

📝 Quick Facts About the One-Time Fee:



It’s only paid once, not monthly or yearly
Covers national induction, access to training, and digital membership
Starting Summer 2025, it will not include merchandise (members can purchase items separately on the merch store)
The Join It registration form will be linked directly on the front page of our website during recruitment season

✅ Chapter Reminders:



Do NOT induct any member who hasn’t paid through the website and been added to Join It
If someone says they paid but isn’t showing up in the system, contact info@estherfundsfoundation.org immediately
Double-check that your team sends the correct link during onboarding
📢 Join It = official national recognition. No fee + no Join It = no membership.

questions.question: Where must the national induction fees be paid?
answers: a) Through the chapter’s Cash App
b) By giving cash to the Membership Chair
c) Through the official Esther Funds Foundation website
d) On Instagram via DMs
correct_answers: c) Through the official Esther Funds Foundation website

questions.question: What is the purpose of Join It?
answers: a) It’s a place to post flyers
b) It tracks event attendance
c) It is EFF’s national membership system that verifies official inducted members
d) It replaces GroupMe
correct_answers: c) It is EFF’s national membership system that verifies official inducted members

questions.question: What will happen starting Summer 2025 regarding national fees?
answers: a) Members will pay monthly
b) The fee will be optional
c) Merchandise will no longer be included with the national induction fee
d) Members will pay their advisors
correct_answers: c) Merchandise will no longer be included with the national induction fee

lessons.title: Lesson 9: Chapter Dues – Supporting Your Local Impact
lessons.markdown:
# 91-lesson-61482847

Chapter Dues – Supporting Your Local Impact

While national dues support EFF’s structure, resources, and certification at the national level, chapter dues are how you fund your impact locally.

These are small, campus-based contributions that help your chapter run smoothly and stay consistent throughout the semester.

💸 What Are Chapter Dues?


Chapter dues are monthly or semester-based fees your executive board sets to help fund everyday chapter needs.

You can collect chapter dues to support:


Snacks for general body meetings
Supplies for events and donation drives
Printed flyers or signage
Small giveaways or welcome gifts for members
Journals, devotionals, or Bibles
✅ These dues stay with your chapter and are managed by your Treasurer.📌 Important Rules About Chapter Dues


✔️ Chapter dues are optional, but recommended ✔️ Must be clearly communicated at interest meetings ✔️ Collected through your chapter’s Cash App, PayPal, or university-approved platform ✔️ Must be tracked in your semester financial report ✔️ Should be affordable (ex: $5–$10/month or $15–$25/semester)

🚫 Chapter Dues Cannot Be Used For:



Gas, personal meals, or reimbursements
Leader gifts or outside group expenses
Anything unrelated to EFF’s mission
Anything not pre-approved by your advisor or not budgeted properly
📣 Pro Tips:



Offer flexibility for students in financial hardship
Use incentives like “dues-paid only” raffles or perks
Assign the Membership Chair or Secretary to track who’s paid
Keep it low-stress and low-cost, but consistent
💬 Chapter dues are your fuel. Use them wisely, track them well, and make sure your members know where the money goes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of chapter dues?
answers: a) To cover national induction into EFF
b) To reimburse board members for gas
c) To help fund local events, meetings, and supplies
d) To give gifts to general body members
correct_answers: c) To help fund local events, meetings, and supplies

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an approved use of chapter dues?
answers: a) Buying snacks for a Bible study
b) Ordering Bibles for new members
c) Paying for the President’s Uber to Target
d) Printing flyers for a donation drive
correct_answers: c) Paying for the President’s Uber to Target

questions.question: What is the best way to collect and manage chapter dues?
answers: a) Through your chapter’s Cash App or approved platform, tracked by the Treasurer
b) Through personal Zelle accounts
c) By giving cash to the Membership Chair
d) Only at the final GBM of the semester
correct_answers: a) Through your chapter’s Cash App or approved platform, tracked by the Treasurer

lessons.title: Module 9 Activity: Build Your Recruitment Week
lessons.markdown:
# 92-lesson-61482941

Build Your Recruitment Period

📍You’ve just been approved to start recruitment. You’re now responsible for welcoming new members into EFF with structure, purpose, and love. Let’s plan it out.

✨ Instructions: Complete Each Section Below


This activity helps you walk through what your Esther’s Experience week could look like — and ensures you’re covering all required parts.

🧠 Step 1: Set the Tone


Theme (Optional): Come up with a title or spiritual theme for your recruitment period (ex: Planted for Purpose, Called & Covered, Every Future Begins Here)

🗓️ Step 2: Build the Experience Schedule


Fill in a possible outline for your recruitment week (use any day names):


Interest Meeting:
Bible Study or Prayer Night:
Service Event (Donation Drive or Outreach):
Bonding or Fun Event:
Workshop or Growth Activity:
Final Induction Planning / Reflection:
✅ Make sure your schedule includes everything required in The Esther’s Experience. You can reference your handbook or the course content.

💬 Step 3: Heart Check


Write 2–3 sentences: How can you make this experience more than just a checklist? What can your chapter do to make students feel welcomed, seen, and spiritually supported?

💡 Remember: this process sets the tone for someone’s entire EFF journey. Lead it with structure, serve with heart.

lessons.title: Module 9 Quiz – Membership, Recruitment & The Esther’s Experience

questions.question: What is the purpose of The Esther’s Experience?

b) To introduce college students to church life

d) To test student commitment through competitive challenges

lessons.title: MODULE 10 – Event Planning, Execution & Chapter Growth
lessons.markdown:
# 94-lesson-61464804

Event Planning, Execution & Chapter Growth

Planning events isn’t just about filling a room. It’s about creating moments that build community, shift culture, and prevent dropouts. This module walks you through what types of events matter, how to plan them well, and how to reflect EFF’s mission in every detail.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Planning with Purpose
lessons.markdown:
# 95-lesson-61464809

Planning with Purpose

Every event your chapter hosts should:


Align with EFF’s mission: dropout prevention, student success, and Christ-centered support
Be accessible and inclusive
Serve a clear purpose (faith, resources, community, or fundraising)
Be well-documented and communicated
Avoid hosting events just because they’re trendy. Focus on impact over popularity.

A “silent study night” with 10 people who stay enrolled is more powerful than a packed event with no purpose.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Required Events Each Semester
lessons.markdown:
# 96-lesson-61464817

Required Events Each Semester

Here’s a reminder of what every chapter is expected to complete:

✅ 2 Bible studies ✅ 2 workshops or educational events ✅ 1 donation drive ✅ 2 general body meetings ✅ 1 scholarship or resource event ✅ 2–3 campus collaborations ✅ Participation in at least 1 national initiative

These events are not optional—they are part of your compliance packet.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Types of EFF-Aligned Events
lessons.markdown:
# 97-lesson-61464823

Types of EFF-Aligned Events

📘 Faith-Based Events


Bible Studies
Prayer Nights
Worship + Word Sessions
Devotional Brunches
🎓 Academic Events


Scholarship Application Parties
Study Halls or Finals Prep Nights
“First-Gen Talks” and Graduation Panels
💜 Community Service


Campus cleanups
Donation drives
Volunteer days with local orgs
🛠️ Workshops


How to Budget in College
Time Management
“I Almost Dropped Out” Panels
Mental Health + Academic Resilience
🎉 Bonding & Culture Building


Game nights
Paint & Praise
Member mixers
Royal Court events

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Event Planning Checklist
lessons.markdown:
# 98-lesson-61464829

Event Planning Checklist

Every event should answer:


What’s the purpose?
What resources do we need?
Who’s leading what?
What’s the promotion plan?
How are we documenting this for compliance?
Before hosting, confirm: ✅ Advisor or university approval ✅ Clear start/end time ✅ GroupMe, social, or email promotion ✅ Roles assigned to each board member ✅ Attendance tracker ready (sign-in sheet or QR code)

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Growth Beyond Events
lessons.markdown:
# 99-lesson-61464836

Growth Beyond Events

Events are just one part of growth. Chapters grow when they:


Build real relationships with members
Stay consistent on social media
Follow up after events
Invite students into leadership
Create space for students to share testimonies and struggles
Collaborate with groups who care about equity, graduation, or student wellness
If you want a packed room, serve your campus with intention.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Staying On Mission – Every Event Has a Purpose
lessons.markdown:
# 100-lesson-61489961

Staying On Mission – Every Event Has a Purpose

Just because an event is fun doesn’t mean it’s fruitful.

As a chapter of Esther Funds Foundation, every event you host should reflect our core mission: 👉 Preventing college dropouts through mentorship, faith, resources, service, and education.

That doesn’t mean everything has to be serious or spiritual — it just means there must be purpose behind it.

🧭 Examples of On-Mission vs. Off-Mission Events


✅ On-Mission Events:



Scholarship Application Night
Finals Prep + Prayer Session
“I Almost Dropped Out” Testimony Panel
Budgeting for Broke College Students Workshop
Donation Drive for Students in Crisis
Mental Health & God Discussion
Faith + Career Vision Board Night
These events are creative, but they equip, encourage, and elevate students.

🚫 Off-Mission Events (and why they don’t work):



Game night with no purpose or connection to EFF values
Talent show with no promotion of student success or dropout prevention
Fashion show with no educational, service, or spiritual tie-in
Any event where the chapter focus shifts toward clout, aesthetics, or hype over impact

🔁 Key Filter: Ask Before You Plan


Before every event, ask your team:

“Does this help a student feel more supported, connected, and likely to graduate?”If yes → plan it with excellence. If no → shift the purpose or let it go.

lessons.title: MODULE 11 – Maximizing Chapter Resources & Tools for Impact
lessons.markdown:
# 102-lesson-61464872

Maximizing Chapter Resources & Tools for Impact

Every Esther Funds Foundation chapter has access to templates, branding materials, trackers, and guides designed to make leadership easier, more professional, and more organized.

But these tools only work if you use them.

This module walks through what’s in the Chapter Resource Hub and how to use it to stay in compliance, grow your chapter, and lead with excellence.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What is the Chapter Resource Hub?
lessons.markdown:
# 103-lesson-61464876

What is the Chapter Resource Hub?

The Chapter Resource Hub is your online folder of tools, templates, and downloads provided by National HQ.

📍 You can access it anytime via: ➡️ www.estherfundsfoundation.org → Chapter Resources

Inside the Hub, you’ll find everything you need to operate your chapter smoothly and stay nationally aligned.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: What You’ll Find in the Resource Hub
lessons.markdown:
# 104-lesson-61464880

What You’ll Find in the Resource Hub

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Why These Resources Matter
lessons.markdown:
# 105-lesson-61464885

Why These Resources Matter

Using these tools helps your chapter:


Stay organized and professional

Keep consistent branding across campuses
Submit strong compliance packets
Save time on design and planning
Avoid confusion and burnout
You don’t have to build from scratch. The foundation is already here—use it.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: How to Keep Chapter Files Organized
lessons.markdown:
# 106-lesson-61464887

How to Keep Chapter Files Organized

To avoid losing track of things:


Create a Google Drive folder labeled: EFF – [Your Chapter Name]

Create subfolders: Events, Photos, Receipts, Compliance, Flyers

Assign one board member to help keep it organized (usually the Secretary, VP, or President)
Backup photos and flyers after every event
Organization reflects stewardship. Don’t wing it—lead with intention.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: When in Doubt, Ask

If you ever feel unsure about:


A design
What form to use
What template is outdated
How to submit your packet

We are here to help you succeed.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Navigating the Chapter Resource Hub – You’re Not On Your Own
lessons.markdown:
# 108-lesson-61490163

Navigating the Chapter Resource Hub – You’re Not On Your Own

Imagine trying to run a full chapter with no structure, no templates, and no direction. We don’t do that here.

The Chapter Resource Hub exists so that every chapter leader has the tools, documents, and guidance needed to lead with excellence. This is where you'll find the things that protect your chapter, keep you compliant, and save you hours of stress.

🧭 Where to Find It


You can access the Chapter Resource Hub anytime at: ➡️ www.estherfundsfoundation.org > Chapter Resources

Bookmark it. Share it. Visit it often. Everything is kept up to date and will be referred to throughout your leadership journey.

📚 Important Documents in the Hub Include:


Here are just a few critical resources you’ll find there:


✅ Collegiate Chapter Event Proposal Templates

✅ Membership Recruitment Guide & Application Templates

✅ National Constitution + Bylaws (2025)

✅ Advisor Information Guide

✅ National Compliance Packet Guide Presentation

✅ Financial Procedures & Volunteer Reporting Tools

✅ Social Media Policy, Branding Guidelines, and Merch Rules

✅ Sample Meeting Agendas & Executive Board Templates

✅ Whistleblower Policy & Conflict of Interest Form

✅ Scholarship Promotion & Ethics Policy

These are NOT optional tools—they are your chapter’s protection plan and professional playbook.

lessons.title: Did you know? Fact 2
lessons.markdown:
# 111-lesson-61478788

Did You Know?

Esther Funds Foundation is the FIRST Christian ministry in the U.S. to establish collegiate chapters focused on preventing college dropouts.

lessons.title: MODULE 12 – College Dropout Prevention: Events, Campaigns & Impact
lessons.markdown:
# 112-lesson-61465089

College Dropout Prevention: Events, Campaigns & Impact

Esther Funds Foundation is here to stop the cycle of college dropout—especially among first-generation, low-income, and underrepresented students.

This module shows you how to design events and campaigns that make real impact, support student retention, and spread awareness of our mission.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Why Students Drop Out of College
lessons.markdown:
# 113-lesson-61465094

Why Students Drop Out of College

Here are just a few reasons students leave school early:


Financial pressure
Housing insecurity
Mental health struggles
Lack of support or mentorship
First-gen navigation stress
Spiritual isolation
Poor academic planning or burnout
EFF events, Bible studies, campaigns, and services are all created to interrupt those risk factors and help students finish strong.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: What EFF Does to Prevent Dropouts
lessons.markdown:
# 114-lesson-61465097

What EFF Does to Prevent Dropouts

As a chapter, your presence on campus can:


Connect students to community
Offer encouragement and faith
Provide financial or emergency resources
Create structure through workshops and meetings
Celebrate students who stay enrolled
Help them believe that graduation is possible

You are a light in the middle of the chaos. Show up with intention.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: High-Impact Events That Prevent Dropouts
lessons.markdown:
# 115-lesson-61465102

High-Impact Events That Prevent Dropouts

These types of events directly support retention:

🧠 Mental Health Check-ins Host safe spaces where students can decompress, journal, pray, or hear testimonies.

💵 Scholarship & FAFSA Nights Walk students through applying for aid or help them research opportunities.

📚 Academic Survival Skills Workshops Teach time management, productivity tips, or study habits.

✝️ Faith-Based Encouragement Events Bible studies that tackle stress, identity, healing, and perseverance.

💬 "I Almost Dropped Out" Panels Let students or alumni share their stories of almost quitting—but finishing.

🤝 Resource Fairs or Collab Days Partner with other orgs to bring campus offices (like counseling, advising, or financial aid) to the students in one room.

📦 Donation Drives for Students Survival kits, emergency groceries, hygiene products—all help reduce dropout pressure.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Campaigns with a Message
lessons.markdown:
# 116-lesson-61465104

Campaigns with a Message

In addition to events, social media campaigns raise awareness and allow your chapter to educate, encourage, and activate your campus.

EFF National Campaigns include:

🚶🏾‍♀️ College Dropout Prevention Walk A public campus walk where members and students wear purple, carry signs, and share facts or testimonies related to college retention. This walk symbolizes the fight to finish, honoring students who stay enrolled against the odds.

📖 Prayer & Purpose Campaign Encourages scripture sharing, prayer posts, and spiritual encouragement for students during tough academic seasons.

👩🏽‍🎓 First-Gen Celebration Week Honors the experiences and strength of first-generation college students through posts, events, and affirmations.

🧠 Mental Health & Faith Series Explores how faith and mental wellness intersect—offering students peace, reflection, and healing.

🏁 Finals Grand Prix A motivational series leading up to finals that encourages students to stay focused, stay prayed up, and finish the semester strong.

Chapters are expected to participate in at least one national campaign per semester. You can also create your own campus campaigns—as long as they align with EFF’s mission.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Putting the Funds in Esther Funds
lessons.markdown:
# 117-lesson-61489833

Let’s be real—money is one of the top reasons students drop out of college.

Many students are just one emergency, one unpaid balance, or one financial mistake away from walking away from school. As an EFF chapter, your job isn’t just to host events. It’s to advocate, educate, and equip students financially.

Here’s How You Can “Put the Funds in Esther Funds”:

✅ Share scholarships regularly Use your chapter’s Instagram to promote active scholarships—especially local ones. Check your school’s scholarship portal weekly and repost opportunities.

✅ Host financial literacy workshops Teach students how to budget, understand their loans, save money, or find emergency resources. Partner with local banks, campus financial aid, or guest speakers.

✅ Create a money-saving resource guide Collect links to campus emergency grants, food pantry hours, textbook swaps, or rent help and make it into a Canva graphic or Linktree page.

✅ Talk about it often Use Bible studies or group discussions to talk about the burden of financial stress—because students need faith and a financial plan.

lessons.title: Module 12 Activity: Prevention in Practice
lessons.markdown:
# 118-lesson-61489847

Prevention in Practice

📍You’ve learned the why—now it’s time to think about the how.

This activity helps you connect everything from this module to what your chapter can actually do on campus to keep students enrolled, supported, and encouraged.

Your Mission: Create a Dropout Prevention Plan

Answer the following prompt in a bulleted list or short sentences:


Name one EFF-style event your chapter could host that directly helps students stay in school. (Example: “Finals Prep & Prayer Night” or “Scholarship Application Party”)💡 The best chapters aren’t just fun—they’re effective. What will your chapter do to leave a real impact?

lessons.title: MODULE 13 – Internal & External Skills of an EFF Leader
lessons.markdown:
# 121-lesson-61465137

Internal & External Skills of an EFF Leader

Being a leader in EFF means more than running events and hosting meetings. It’s about who you are behind the scenes, how you carry yourself in public, and how you lead people both spiritually and professionally.

This module breaks down the two areas every leader must grow in:


Internal (spiritual & emotional)
External (professional & practical)

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Internal Skills – The Heart of a Leader
lessons.markdown:
# 122-lesson-61465144

Internal Skills – The Heart of a Leader

These are the skills that no one sees at first, but everyone feels over time.

🧠 Emotional Intelligence Being able to lead without reacting out of emotion. Practicing patience, empathy, and self-awareness in stressful moments.

🕊️ Humility Knowing when to admit you don’t know everything. Leading from a place of service, not ego.

💭 Accountability Owning your responsibilities, following through, and letting others correct you without defensiveness.

🕯️ Integrity Doing what’s right even when it’s hard or when no one’s watching. Keeping your word. Representing EFF and Christ with consistency.

🕊️ Resilience Not quitting when things get hard. Leading with endurance through conflict, disorganization, or disappointment.

📿 Spiritual Discernment Praying before reacting. Leaning on God’s wisdom, not just logic or popularity. Being sensitive to the atmosphere in your chapter.

Internal skills are the foundation. If your inner world is messy, your leadership will be too.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: External Skills – How You Carry the Mission
lessons.markdown:
# 123-lesson-61465150

External Skills – How You Carry the Mission

These are the skills you’ll use in real-time as a public representative of EFF.

🎤 Public Speaking & Communication Being able to speak with clarity, energy, and conviction—whether you're welcoming people to a GBM or presenting to a university department.

📁 Event Planning & Execution Knowing how to build a timeline, assign roles, and prepare for what could go wrong. Hosting doesn’t mean chaos—it means order.

📸 Social Media & Branding Awareness Understanding the EFF look, tone, and voice. Representing the brand well in posts, flyers, and captions.

🤝 Collaboration & Delegation Working with other orgs, not taking over. Knowing how to delegate tasks to avoid burnout and build team trust.

💰 Fundraising Strategy Promoting fundraisers with purpose, explaining why donations matter, and communicating impact clearly.

🧭 Navigating Campus Culture Understanding what your students need and what your campus climate looks like. Making EFF relevant but still aligned with our values.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: What Real Leadership Looks Like
lessons.markdown:
# 124-lesson-61465160

What Real Leadership Looks Like

Leadership isn’t about being loud. It’s about being anchored.

You lead when you:


Pray before responding
Stay calm under pressure
Show up when no one else does
Correct your peers with love, not ego
Make others feel seen, heard, and valued
Represent EFF even when you’re off the clock
People are watching how you:


Treat your board
Post online
Respond to conflict
Carry yourself after the event is over
You may be the only example of God’s love some students see. Let your leadership be salt and light.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Accountability in Leadership – Holding the Standard
lessons.markdown:
# 125-lesson-61482975

Accountability in Leadership – Holding the Standard

In EFF, leadership isn’t just about having a title—it’s about consistently showing up, growing, and honoring the call.

That’s why accountability is not optional. It protects the health of your chapter, the trust of your members, and the strength of your leadership team.

🧩 Why Accountability Matters


Without accountability, leaders:


Stop showing up or serving with excellence
Create division, confusion, and resentment
Damage the mission and ministry environment
With accountability, leaders:


Stay aligned with expectations
Grow personally and spiritually
Build trust with each other and the general body
Create a culture of consistency and honor
🟪 Accountability Within the Executive Board


Here’s how to keep your board focused and responsible:

✅ Hold monthly or biweekly board check-ins ✅ Review leadership goals and progress ✅ Be honest when someone is slipping—address issues early ✅ Recommend a Leadership Expectations Contract (sample can be found in Chapter Resources) ✅ Use probation periods for leaders who fall short but are willing to grow

📌 You can love someone and still hold them accountable. That’s biblical and that’s leadership.
🟨 Using Leadership Contracts & Probation Periods


Leadership Contracts (recommended):


Outline expectations (attendance, behavior, communication, spiritual tone)
Signed by each executive board member
Reviewed at mid-semester check-in
Probation Periods (used with grace):


2–4 weeks of accountability after repeated issues
Set goals, check progress weekly, and make decisions after
Can lead to restoration—or replacement if needed
These tools protect the health of the board without shame—they create structure, honor, and alignment.

🟩 Accountability for General Body Members


While your general body isn’t held to the same standard as your board, they should still experience structure and follow-through.

How to keep them accountable:


Track attendance and engagement
Communicate expectations clearly during recruitment
Provide growth opportunities (teams, prayer roles, support roles)
Offer grace—but call out disrespect, inconsistency, or toxicity early
Celebrate their wins publicly!
💬 You can be a welcoming chapter and still have structure. That’s how you grow—and last.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Final Things to Know Before the Exam
lessons.markdown:
# 126-lesson-61465184

Final Things to Know Before the Exam

Before you take the final exam and officially become a certified EFF leader, there are a few key reminders that will carry you through your semester:

⏱️ Time Management Matters


You're a student. You’re busy. But your leadership role is still real.

Make time for:


E-board meetings
Prepping for events
Following up with members
Submitting reports and updates
Use calendars, reminders, and shared folders to stay on top of your role. Don’t lead from a place of chaos—lead from a place of structure.

🤝 Conflict Resolution Starts With Maturity


Issues will come up. People will misunderstand each other. You might even disagree with your board or advisor. That’s normal.

But here’s what EFF leadership does NOT do:


Gossip in group chats
Publicly bash someone online
Split the chapter into sides
Go around leadership structure
Instead:


Talk directly.
Use “I” statements.
Invite your advisor or liaison into the conversation if needed.
Pray before reacting.
Mature leaders handle conflict with grace and truth.

📬 Stay Connected to National HQ


You’re not alone. National HQ is here to support you.

We ask that you:


Check in with your chapter liaison (if assigned)
Read messages in the group chat
Submit compliance packets and updates on time
Email info@estherfundsfoundation.org with any issues or questions
We are here to help you win—not micromanage you. Let’s work as a team.

🧭 Keep Your Events on Mission


Just because something is popular doesn’t mean it’s purposeful.

Ask before every event:


Does this reflect Christ?
Does this align with EFF’s mission?
Will this help someone stay in school or grow in faith?
Keep your events: ✅ Impact-driven ✅ Spirit-led ✅ Organized ✅ Documented ✅ Beautifully branded

🌱 Grow Your Internal & External Leadership Skills Daily


You now know the two sides of leadership:


Internal: integrity, emotional maturity, prayer life, humility
External: public speaking, planning, branding, communication
Work on both. Every week. Every semester.

If you only grow one and ignore the other, your leadership will always be off balance.

🔑 Final Thought:

You’re about to be certified. That means something.

You’re saying: “I’m ready to serve.” “I’m willing to lead with excellence.” “I’m committed to helping students stay in school and walk with God.”

Take a breath. Pray. And let’s finish strong.

lessons.title: Mini Activity: Accountability in Action
lessons.markdown:
# 127-lesson-61483017

Mini Activity: Accountability in Action

📍Every great leader needs both grace and structure. Read the situations below and choose how you would respond as a mature, Spirit-led leader.

🧩 Scenario 1: The Disconnected Vice President


Your VP has missed 3 meetings, hasn’t responded in the group chat, and forgot to turn in their portion of the event plan. They’re going through personal stuff but haven’t communicated.

What’s your next move? 

a) Remove them from their position without warning b) Gossip to other board members about how frustrating it is c) Set up a 1-on-1 conversation and recommend a short-term accountability plan

questions.question: Scenario 2: A GBM Member Shows Up Disruptive A new member keeps arriving late, talking over others, and making jokes during prayer time. It's starting to affect the culture. How do you handle it?
answers: a) Ignore it and hope it stops
b) Call them out in front of the whole group
c) Pull them aside, affirm them, and lovingly explain expectations
correct_answers: c) Pull them aside, affirm them, and lovingly explain expectations

lessons.title: Quiz
lessons.markdown:
# 128-lesson-61465275

Quick Tip Before You Go: Accountability isn’t about being harsh — it’s about protecting the mission and the people.

No GroupMe explosions. No shady side-eyes. No power trips. Just real leaders doing what they said they’d do.

 AND GOOD NEWS: There’s NO quiz for this one. But don’t get too comfortable — we’re still watching 👀

lessons.title: Did know you? Fact #4
lessons.markdown:
# 129-lesson-61478831

Did You Know?

Esther Funds Foundation is working toward launching an emergency grant called the Esther Emergency Grant Fund? 💡

This future grant will help students facing real crises—whether it’s unexpected tuition balances, emergency housing, groceries, or transportation needs.

lessons.title: FINAL EXAM OVERVIEW – You Made It!
lessons.markdown:
# 130-lesson-61465353

🎉 Congratulations, Leader.


You’ve officially completed all 13 modules of the Esther Funds Foundation National Leadership Course.

You’ve walked through:


What EFF is and why we exist
How to lead with faith and structure
How to host impactful, mission-aligned events
What’s required for compliance and reporting
What it means to steward a chapter—not just run one
How to protect your personal character while representing a national brand
And now… you’re almost done.

✅ What’s Next? The Final Exam.


To complete your certification, you must:


Pass the Final Exam with an 85% or higher

Review any answers you miss (you’ll get 2 retake chances if needed)
Once passed, your name will be submitted to HQ
Your Certificate of Completion will be emailed to you
You’ll be added to the official EFF National Leader Registry

📝 Final Exam Details:



30–40 multiple choice and scenario-based questions
Covers all 13 modules
Open-note, but time-limited (if you're enforcing that via platform)
Be prepared to apply what you’ve learned, not just remember facts
⏳ Important Reminder:


You must complete the course within 14 days of your leadership session unless you’ve been granted an extension. If you need more time due to disability, personal hardship, or academic accommodations, email us at: 📧 info@estherfundsfoundation.org Subject: Leadership Course Extension Request

🕊️ Final Words Before You Begin:

Take a moment to breathe. You’ve already shown excellence by getting this far.

Pray for clarity. Lead with confidence. And remember:

You are not just representing a chapter. You’re carrying a mantle to build, serve, and lead “for such a time as this.”We’re proud of you. Let’s finish what we started.

lessons.title: Esther Funds Foundation National Collegiate Leadership Certification Exam
lessons.markdown:
# 131-lesson-61465355

Esther Funds Foundation National Collegiate Leadership Certification Final Exam

📜 Instructions:


This is your final exam to become a certified E-board leader of Esther Funds Foundation.
All questions are multiple choice. Select the best answer for each.
You must earn an 85% or higher to pass.
Submit through your designated Teachable platform or chapter training portal.
You will be issued a certificate upon completion,
Once you receive your certificate you have permission to share on any social media platform.

courses.title: Pretty Girls Who Serve National Leadership Training: Building Sisterhood, Leadership, and Impact
courses.description:
The Pretty Girls Who Serve National Leadership Training Course is the official training program for all National Executive Board members, national leaders, and Royal Court leaders serving within the Pretty Girls Who Serve initiative under the Esther Funds Foundation.

This course exists to fully equip women leaders to operate, expand, and steward Pretty Girls Who Serve as a national sisterhood rooted in service, faith, leadership, and excellence. Leaders who complete this course will be prepared to independently manage membership growth, oversee chapter-level programming, lead women-centered events, foster authentic sisterhood, and uphold the values and standards of Esther Funds Foundation.

This training is required for all women serving in leadership roles within Pretty Girls Who Serve, including national officers, program directors, chapter-facing leaders, and members of the Miss Pretty Girls Who Serve Royal Court.

modules.title: Next Steps — Let’s Get to Work, Pretty Girl

lessons.title: Welcome, Sister
lessons.markdown:
# 01-lesson-64234922

Welcome to Pretty Girls Who Serve leadership.

If you are here, it means you were seen as someone who can lead women with care, responsibility, and intention. PGWS leadership is not JUST about titles. It is about stewardship. We build women. We support EFF chapters. We move with alignment.

This course authorizes you to operate in your PGWS national leadership role. You are not expected to know everything yet. You are expected to stay aligned, ask questions, and lead with integrity.

Everything within PGWS operates under Esther Funds Foundation Nationals. This protects our sisterhood and allows us to grow safely and sustainably.

questions.question: Pretty Girls Who Serve operates under which entity?
answers: A. Individual chapters
B. Independent leadership
C. Esther Funds Foundation Nationals
D. Social media leadership
correct_answers: C. Esther Funds Foundation Nationals

questions.question: What does completing this course authorize you to do?
answers: A. Create PGWS chapters independently
B. Operate in your PGWS national leadership role
C. Fundraise without approval
D. Skip national meetings
correct_answers: B. Operate in your PGWS national leadership role

lessons.title: The History of Pretty Girls Who Serve
lessons.markdown:
# 02-lesson-64234988

Pretty Girls Who Serve was created to provide women within Esther Funds Foundation a dedicated sisterhood space where faith, service, leadership, and femininity meet.

PGWS began with intentional experiences such as brunches, sisterhood gatherings, national challenges, and the Miss Pretty Girls Who Serve Scholarship Competition. These experiences shaped PGWS into a structured, national sisterhood designed to grow across campuses and cities.

PGWS was created to last, scale, and unify women nationally.

questions.question: Why was Pretty Girls Who Serve created?
answers: A. To replace Esther Funds Foundation
B. To provide intentional sisterhood for women and improve EFF member bonding and engagement
C. To operate as a short-term program
D. To focus only on merchandise
correct_answers: B. To provide intentional sisterhood for women and improve EFF member bonding and engagement

questions.question: Which experiences helped shape PGWS early on?
answers: A. Academic conferences only
B. Brunches, gatherings, and national initiatives
C. Athletic competitions
D. Study halls
correct_answers: B. Brunches, gatherings, and national initiatives

lessons.title: Mission & Vision of PGWS
lessons.markdown:
# 03-lesson-64234998

Mission 

Pretty Girls Who Serve exists to cultivate a national sisterhood of college and post-graduate women who love Jesus and are committed to serving with excellence. Powered by Esther Funds Foundation, PGWS develops women leaders and supports consistent national programming.

Vision PGWS envisions a nationally recognized sisterhood where women discover their beauty in Christ, lead confidently, and serve purposefully across campuses and beyond.

questions.question: PGWS exists primarily to:
answers: A. Host social events
B. Cultivate faith-centered sisterhood and leadership
C. Operate independently
D. Replace chapter leadership
correct_answers: B. Cultivate faith-centered sisterhood and leadership

questions.question: PGWS supports Esther Funds Foundation by:
answers: A. Competing with chapters
B. Strengthening leadership, belonging, and engagement
C. Focusing only on branding
D. Eliminating structure
correct_answers: B. Strengthening leadership, belonging, and engagement

lessons.title: PGWS Identity, Colors & Branding
lessons.markdown:
# 04-lesson-64235001

Pretty Girls Who Serve maintains a consistent national identity.

Official Colors: Hot Pink and White Official Merchandise: All PGWS merchandise is sold exclusively through the Esther Funds Foundation online store.

Leadership Requirements


All National Executive Board members must order a PGWS T-shirt
All National Executive Board members must order a PGWS Executive Board polo
All leaders must maintain active PGWS National Membership

# 05-lesson-64235003

PGWS operates as a national sisterhood. All members are PGWS National Members.

Once per semester, PGWS Nationals host a virtual induction ceremony to welcome new sisters. All inducted members receive a PGWS National Membership Certificate.

Induction reinforces belonging, expectations, and unity across campuses.

# 06-lesson-64235007

PGWS leadership is active.

Each month, leaders will:

Host one virtual sisterhood bonding event
Support chapters hosting PGWS brunches
Maintain the PGWS national calendar
All leaders receive official PGWS email addresses and are expected to check them regularly.

# 07-lesson-64235013

PGWS must host at least one national workshop per semester. Leaders also support chapters as they host PGWS brunches and adopt PGWS into their programming.

Every chapter must appoint a Director of Sisterhood Initiatives to lead women-centered programming locally.

PGWS National Leadership will also plan one large national PGWS meetup.

All fundraising and initiatives must be approved by Esther Funds Foundation Nationals.

lessons.markdown:
# 08-lesson-64235015

PGWS hosts one Miss Pretty Girls Who Serve Scholarship Competition per year.

The competition highlights service-driven women, supports scholarship fundraising, and selects Royal Court ambassadors who represent PGWS nationally.

Royal Court members are leaders and ambassadors of sisterhood.

lessons.title: Sisterhood & Being a Good Sister
lessons.markdown:
# 09-lesson-64235017

Sisterhood is the foundation of PGWS.

Being a good sister means supporting rather than competing, communicating with care, addressing conflict maturely, and creating safe spaces for women.

Leadership requires emotional maturity and grace.

questions.question: Being a good sister includes:
answers: A. Competition
B. Gossip
C. Support and accountability
D. Avoiding responsibility
correct_answers: C. Support and accountability

questions.question: How should conflict be handled?
answers: A. Publicly
B. Ignored
C. Maturely and privately
D. Through social media
correct_answers: C. Maturely and privately

lessons.title: Christ-Centered Sisterhood (Bible Study)
lessons.markdown:
# 10-lesson-64235019

PGWS is rooted in faith.

Key Scripture: Ecclesiastes 4:9–10 “Two are better than one… If either of them falls down, one can help the other up.”

This scripture reminds us that sisterhood is about support, not isolation. We lift one another when leadership feels heavy and celebrate one another when victories come.

Faith anchors our leadership, our compassion, and our service.

questions.question: The scripture emphasizes:
answers: A. Individual success
B. Competition
C. Support and partnership
D. Independence
correct_answers: C. Support and partnership

questions.question: How does faith strengthen PGWS leadership?
answers: A. It replaces structure
B. It anchors leadership with purpose and care
C. It removes accountability
D. It limits growth
correct_answers: B. It anchors leadership with purpose and care

lessons.title: National Executive Board Roles & National Operations
lessons.markdown:
# 11-lesson-64235221

How the PGWS National Executive Board Works

Pretty Girls Who Serve is designed to scale nationally, which means leadership must be structured, accountable, and unified.

The National Executive Board works together to run the entire PGWS program. While each role has a lane, no role operates in isolation. Collaboration, communication, and respect are required at all times.

Leadership Flow (Important):


The National President oversees the entire PGWS program
The National President reports to the Executive Director of Esther Funds Foundation

The Executive Board reports to and supports the National President
All Executive Board members:


Meet once per month

Are expected to complete assigned tasks on time
Are held accountable through a strike system

National President

The National President oversees all aspects of Pretty Girls Who Serve.

This role includes:


Leading and directing the PGWS National Executive Board
Overseeing programming, membership, events, branding, and growth
Ensuring all PGWS initiatives align with Esther Funds Foundation standards
Serving as the primary point of contact between PGWS and EFF leadership
Reviewing progress, addressing challenges, and guiding national vision
The National President does not do everything alone. She leads, delegates, follows up, and ensures accountability.

Vice Presidents (2 Positions)

The Vice Presidents assist and support the National President.

Responsibilities include:


Helping oversee daily operations of PGWS
Supporting execution of national initiatives
Stepping in when the National President is unavailable
Helping ensure executive board members stay on task
Vice Presidents help keep the program running smoothly and act as support pillars within leadership.

Secretary (National Calendar & Documentation)

The Secretary is responsible for organization and structure.

Responsibilities include:


Managing the PGWS National Calendar

Recording notes from monthly executive board meetings
Tracking deadlines, initiatives, and meeting schedules
Reporting directly to the National President
The Secretary ensures leadership stays organized and informed.

Fundraising Chair(s)

The Fundraising Chair(s) help raise funds for Esther Funds Foundation Nationals through PGWS initiatives.

Responsibilities include:


Developing PGWS fundraising ideas
Submitting fundraising proposals for approval
Supporting approved PGWS fundraisers
Collaborating with the National President on fundraising goals
Fundraising cannot move forward without national approval.

Historian (Data, Tracking & Feedback)

The Historian plays a critical role in growth and evaluation.

Responsibilities include:


Tracking all PGWS initiatives, including chapter-level PGWS activities
Recording data from events, brunches, workshops, and challenges
Collecting feedback from chapters and members
Maintaining records that show PGWS impact and growth
The Historian helps leadership understand what is working and where improvement is needed.

Events & Brunch Advisor Chair

This role oversees PGWS brunches and women-centered events.

Responsibilities include:


Supporting chapters that host PGWS brunches
Helping coordinate brunch timelines and expectations
Ensuring brunches include:
Beauty-themed activities
Pink-themed decor or elements
Intentional bonding moments


Serving as a support contact for Directors of Sisterhood Initiatives at chapters
This role should actively reach out to chapter leaders to ensure they feel supported.

Membership Coordinator

The Membership Coordinator oversees all women in PGWS.

Responsibilities include:


Managing PGWS membership questions
Supporting virtual inductions once per semester

Ensuring all members receive PGWS National Membership Certificates

Overseeing membership pricing:
$45 for women joining both EFF and PGWS
$15 for women already EFF members joining PGWS


Ensuring all PGWS members are registered in Join It

If you ever need to look up a PGWS member, Join It is the system.

Social Media & Branding Chair

This role manages PGWS’s public presence.

Responsibilities include:


Posting weekly PGWS flyers and scriptures

Planning PGWS social media initiatives
Promoting women-centered and PGWS initiatives
Maintaining branding consistency with hot pink and white
Coordinating closely with the PGWS Ambassador President
This role ensures PGWS is visible, polished, and aligned online.

Community Service Chair

Responsibilities include:


Developing PGWS service initiatives
Supporting chapters with service opportunities
Aligning service with PGWS values and EFF mission
Chaplain (Faith-Based Leadership)

The Chaplain oversees the spiritual health of PGWS.

Responsibilities include:


Leading PGWS Bible studies
Coordinating prayer initiatives
Offering spiritual encouragement and support
Supporting leaders and members through faith-based guidance
Wellness & Sisterhood Coordinator

This role protects the emotional health of the sisterhood.

Responsibilities include:


Checking in on sisters in the group chat multiple times per week
Hosting safe spaces for women to vent and feel supported
Supporting sisterhood bonding and wellness initiatives
Helping de-escalate tension and encourage healthy communication
PGWS Ambassador President

This role oversees PGWS ambassadors and visibility.

Responsibilities include:


Assigning monthly ambassador tasks

Coordinating video creation and promotion
Working closely with the Social Media & Branding Chair
Supporting promotion of PGWS initiatives and events
Ambassadors help PGWS reach new women nationally.

lessons.title: Equity, Presentation, Conduct & Accountability
lessons.markdown:
# 12-lesson-64235266

How We Treat Each Other Matters

Pretty Girls Who Serve leadership is built on equity, respect, and professionalism.

Leaders are expected to:


Treat one another with respect at all times
Never gossip about another leader or member
Address concerns privately and appropriately
Represent PGWS with maturity and grace
Presentation matters. How you speak, act, and show up reflects PGWS and Esther Funds Foundation.

The Strike System & Accountability

PGWS leadership operates under a strike system.

A strike may be issued if:


You fail to complete assigned tasks
You miss meetings without communication
You do not meet deadlines
You are disrespectful, gossip, or act unprofessionally
Important:


If you cannot attend a meeting or meet a deadline, you must communicate in advance
Repeated strikes may result in removal from leadership

Accountability protects the sisterhood and ensures everyone is carrying their weight.

correct_answers: C. A strike

correct_answers: C. To ensure accountability and fairness

lessons.title: Final Reminder
lessons.markdown:
# 13-lesson-64235283

FINAL REMINDER: Leadership Standards & Decision-Making

Before you officially step into action, please remember these non-negotiable leadership practices for Pretty Girls Who Serve.

Meeting Minutes Are Required

All PGWS National Executive Board meetings must have minutes taken.

Minutes should clearly document:
Attendance
Topics discussed
Decisions made
Action items and deadlines

Meeting minutes protect the sisterhood, keep leadership aligned, and ensure accountability. The Secretary is responsible for recording and maintaining these minutes, but all leaders are expected to review them.

Executive Board Voting Is Required

Before any event, initiative, or program is added to the PGWS calendar:


The idea must be discussed during an Executive Board meeting
The Executive Board must vote on the event or initiative
Only approved items may be adopted onto the national calendar
Voting ensures shared ownership, fairness, and alignment across leadership.

Why This Matters

These practices:


Keep PGWS organized and professional
Prevent confusion or last-minute changes
Ensure all leaders have a voice
Support national growth and sustainability
Leadership is not just creativity. It’s structure, communication, and shared decision-making.

correct_answers: C. The Executive Board discusses and votes on it

lessons.title: Next Steps — Let’s Get to Work, Pretty Girl
lessons.markdown:
# 14-lesson-64235277

You did it. You’ve completed the Pretty Girls Who Serve National Leadership onboarding, and we are so excited to have you officially step into this sisterhood and leadership space.

Pretty Girls Who Serve is the official sisterhood branch of Esther Funds Foundation, and now it’s time to move from training into action.

Below are your immediate next steps as a PGWS National Executive Board leader.

1. Attend Your First PGWS National Executive Board Meeting

Your first official step is to join or host your first PGWS National Executive Board meeting.

This meeting sets the tone for:


How you work together as a team
Communication expectations
Planning priorities for the semester
Come prepared, on time, and ready to collaborate.

2. Build the PGWS Monthly & Semester Calendar

As a national team, you will:


Build the PGWS shared national calendar

Plan monthly touchpoints

Remember that PGWS must host at least one national workshop per semester

The shared calendar is essential. It keeps everyone aligned, avoids overlap, and helps PGWS scale smoothly.

3. Plan Sisterhood Bonding Moments

Sisterhood does not have to be overwhelming to be effective.

Your goal is to plan:


2–3 intentional sisterhood bonding moments per semester
These can be virtual, relaxed, and welcoming
Focus on connection, encouragement, and community
Quality over quantity always.

4. Strengthen PGWS Programming & Visibility

PGWS leadership should focus on:


Building women-centered initiatives
Creating meaningful PGWS programming
Supporting a strong and consistent PGWS social media presence

This includes sharing flyers, scriptures, sisterhood initiatives, and national moments that reflect PGWS values.

5. Check In on Chapters With PGWS Programs

One of the most important parts of your role is chapter support.

You should:


Check in on chapters that already have PGWS programs
Offer guidance, encouragement, and resources
Support chapters hosting PGWS brunches and initiatives
6. Encourage Chapters to Adopt PGWS

The purpose of PGWS is to support chapter membership engagement and retention.

Encourage chapters to adopt PGWS because:


Strong sisterhood increases retention
Women stay involved when they feel connected
PGWS gives chapters structured women-centered programming
This is how PGWS grows nationally and sustainably.

7. Become an Official PGWS National Member

If you have not already done so:


Complete your PGWS National Membership

Ensure you are registered in Join It

All PGWS leaders are expected to be active national members.

8. Order Your PGWS Apparel

All National Executive Board members should:


Purchase their PGWS T-shirt

Purchase their PGWS Executive Board polo

These are worn at meetings, events, and official PGWS moments. Presentation matters.

9. Bond With Your Sisters

Take time to:


Get to know your fellow leaders
Build genuine connections
Support one another beyond tasks
Strong leadership teams are built on trust and sisterhood

10. Share the Sisterhood

PGWS needs your gifts, creativity, and talent.

Help spread the sisterhood by:


Sharing PGWS initiatives
Inviting women into community
Showing up fully in your role
You were chosen for a reason.

courses.title: Rooted: Prepare to Charter an EFF Chapter A Pre-Training Course for Chartering Presidents · Blossom & Impact Leadership Academy
courses.description:
Before you build a chapter, you must become the leader who can carry it. Rooted is where that begins.

This pre-training course prepares Chartering Presidents to bring Esther Funds Foundation to their campus. It isn't about recruiting or events yet — it's about preparation, character, and leadership. You'll get to know EFF deeply, sharpen your "why," grow into a servant leader who sets a healthy culture (not a power trip), define your chapter's mission and vision, and plan the chapter you're called to start.

By the end, you'll have completed your charter application and a guided Chapter Plan — your readiness proof for your interview with Nationals. We walk with you every step.

Build the roots first. Everything else grows from here. For such a time as this. Every Future Fulfilled. 🕊️

modules.title: MODULE 2 — What Is Esther Funds Foundation?
MODULE 4/5 — Your Why & Your Calling & Character & Leadership: Lead Without the Power Trip
MODULE 5/6-Character & Leadership: Lead Without the Power Trip & Leadership Building
MODULE 7/8— Define Your Chapter's Mission & Vision & Executive Board
Module 9/10-Ground Rules & Complete Your Charter Application & Next Steps

What Rooted is: a short prep course to get you ready — your character, your "why," and your plan — before you ever recruit or launch. Build the foundation first; everything else grows from it.

lessons.title: How the course works
lessons.markdown:
# 03-lesson-66197211

How the course works

How it works: move through the modules in order. Each has a short lesson and a quick quiz. Finish this course and your charter application before your interview.

lessons.title: The ground rules — and why they exist
lessons.markdown:
# 04-lesson-66197215

Ground rules (right now): You're not official yet. ✅ DO: learn, grow, plan, complete your application, build interest with the approved flyer. 🚫 DON'T yet: recruit a board, host events, post graphics, collect money, or start socials/an email. (These protect you and EFF legally and reputationally.)

lessons.title: The road ahead
lessons.markdown:
# 05-lesson-66197217

The road: Apply → Rooted + Capstone → Interview → Approved → recruit board & advisor → register → set foundation → chartered

lessons.title: Module 1: Prepare to Lead
lessons.markdown:
# 06-lesson-66200656

Leadership in EFF is stewardship, not status. A steward cares for something valuable that isn't theirs — and you're entrusted with students' well-being, real money, and a ministry's name. That means serving with humility, leading with integrity, empowering others, and owning your mistakes. Like Nehemiah, you became a leader the moment a broken thing became your burden to carry — not when you got a title. So we prepare before we build: you can't give what you don't have, and the chapter will become whatever you become first. Pray first, plan well, persevere always.

lessons.title: What Is Esther Funds Foundation?
lessons.markdown:
# 07-lesson-66200752

EFF is a Christ-centered nonprofit that prevents college dropouts among minority, first-gen, and low-income students — founded Jan 23, 2024 by Shayna Vincent, who dropped out twice before finishing. Here's the key: students rarely drop out from lack of ability. They drop out because life hits a gap — money, housing, mental health, no roadmap — at a moment no one's there to catch them. Your chapter is that net. EFF runs on 4 pillars (Chapter Expansion, Scholarships, Faith-Based Support, Advocacy) and initiatives like the R.E.A.C.H. Project (replaced James Pillar), Esther's Light, and the Lift Fund, plus two branches (Black Girls Read and Rise; Pretty Girls Who Serve). A collegiate chapter is the student-led local arm of EFF on a campus — not an independent club. Everything a chapter does has one purpose: keeping a student in school.

lessons.title: MODULE 3 — The Chapter Lifecycle
lessons.markdown:
# 08-lesson-66200765

Every healthy chapter moves through the same repeating cycle, so nothing surprises you: Charter & Launch → Recruit & Onboard → Operate → Serve the Mission → Stay Compliant → Grow & Sustain → Transition. It's a cycle, not a line — officer terms are one year, so leadership hands off and the cycle renews. Your job isn't just to run the chapter this year; it's to build something strong enough to be passed on and outlive you.

lessons.title: Your Why & Your Calling
lessons.markdown:
# 09-lesson-66200770

Motivation runs out; a clear "why" doesn't. People follow conviction, not titles — so before you lead anyone, get honest about why you're doing this. Count the cost now (time, energy, hard conversations) so you don't quit halfway. And remember: your own struggles are your fuel and your credibility. "Who knows but that you have come to your position for such a time as this?" (Esther 4:14).

lessons.title: Character & Leadership: Lead Without the Power Trip
lessons.markdown:
# 10-lesson-66200801

The #1 thing that destroys chapters is a leader on a power trip — using authority to serve themselves. In a volunteer community held together only by trust, ego dissolves the glue and people leave. So flip it: authority is for serving, not controlling. Watch the warning signs in yourself (deciding alone, needing credit, favoritism, defensiveness). Remember: your character becomes the culture — people copy what you model. And consult, don't command — shared decisions are better decisions and build a team that will run through walls for you.

The #1 thing that destroys chapters is a leader on a power trip — using authority to serve themselves. In a volunteer community held together only by trust, ego dissolves the glue and people leave. So flip it: authority is for serving, not controlling. Watch the warning signs in yourself (deciding alone, needing credit, favoritism, defensiveness). Remember: your character becomes the culture — people copy what you model. And consult, don't command — shared decisions are better decisions and build a team that will run through walls for you.

lessons.title: Leadership Building
lessons.markdown:
# 13-lesson-66211653

Leaders are built, not born — leadership is a set of skills you grow, not a personality you're stuck with. Commit to growing in: communication (listen more than you talk), delegation (entrust real responsibility), time management, conflict resolution (go directly & privately — Matthew 18), emotional intelligence, vision-casting, developing others, and teachability (the master skill — stay coachable). You don't have to master these yet; you just have to keep growing. Your chapter will never rise higher than your leadership.

lessons.title: Define Your Chapter's Mission & Vision
lessons.markdown:
# 15-lesson-66211663

Mission = what you do now; Vision = where you're going. They're not a box to check — they're the tool you'll use every week to decide what's worth your time and to keep the chapter from drifting into a purposeless social club. Write them down (a feeling in your head can't align a board or filter a decision). Make your mission flow from EFF's (dropout prevention through faith + support) and root it in your campus's real needs and real students. Aim for clear and true, not fancy.

lessons.title: Know & Choose a Strong Board
lessons.markdown:
# 16-lesson-66211665

Your biggest decision is who's on your board — and most leaders fail by picking friends. Choose on character and role-fit, not friendship, because a friend who underperforms endangers both the role and the friendship, and you can't easily remove people once they're on. Match the right person to the right role (a disorganized friend isn't your Treasurer). Know the 11 roles, watch red flags (in it for the title, gossips, can't take feedback, unreliable), and avoid cliques. Choose carefully up front — it saves a semester of drama.
